question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any services and supports his Department is providing to the homeless in Belfast.
answer_text: My Department provides a range of services and supports to people experiencing homelessness. The Public Health Agency commissions the Belfast Inclusion Health Service, offering communitybased multidisciplinary services such as dental, podiatry, mental health, health screening, vaccination administration and social work support, for those experiencing homelessness in Belfast.    The Complex Lives initiative, delivered in conjunction with the Public Health Agency, Belfast City Council and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, offers a multidisciplinary, wholesystem model of care for those facing severe and multiple disadvantages, including homelessness. My Department also leads on several nonaccommodationfocused actions under the Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan and continues to work to address the wider mental health, substance use and healthinequality issues which are linked to homelessness.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of judicial reviews his Department are currently involved in; (ii) the nature of his Department's involvement in any judicial review; and (iii) the cost of any involvement in these judicial reviews.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any services; and supports her Department is providing to the homeless in Belfast.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“In Belfast the Housing Executive funds a number of services to support the homeless.The Welcome Organisation provides Street Outreach services to address and prevent rough sleeping and support those whose health and well-being is compromised, or individuals at risk of losing accommodation due to street activity and associated anti-social behaviour.The Welcome Support Hub provides a range of services to help people move on from homelessness, as well to get their basic needs met, for example, meals and health assessments.  The Extern Multi-Disciplinary Homeless Support Team which works with people with complex needs through the provision of floating support to maintain tenancies, specialist social work support and risk assessments, crisis intervention, and access to statutory supports.Since October 2021, the Housing Executive has supported the delivery of Complex Lives which provides coordinated, multi-agency support for people experiencing chronic homelessness who present with a range of complex needs. The model seeks to remove barriers to treatment, improve access to health support, and stabilise individuals’ outcomes across accommodation, health and well-being and offending.In addition to the services above the Housing Executive also supports a range of accommodation based and floating support services delivered via Supporting People funding.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any legislation (i) his Department will not bring forward; and (ii) that will be brought forward and completed, before the end of the 2022-27 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: During this Assembly mandate, it was my intention to bring forward the following array of Bills: -Adult Protection;Hospital Parking Charges;Safe & Effective Staffing;Control of Data Processing Act;Duty of Candour;Minimum Unit Price of Alcohol;Pharmacy Profession Regulation Bill;Mental Health and Capacity Bill; andPublic Health BillThe Adult Protection Bill was formally introduced to the Assembly on 17 June 2025 and is currently with the Committee for Health for scrutiny.The Hospital Parking Charges Bill was introduced to the Assembly on Monday 9 March 2026. The Final Stage is currently scheduled to take place on Monday 20 April 2026.  This will allow just 15 working days to achieve Royal Assent in advance of 12 May 2026, when charges for hospital car parking will be abolished if the legislation is not amended.  I am hoping to introduce the Safe and Effective Staffing Bill to the Assembly in September 2026, and the Organisational Duty of Candour Bill in October 2026.Unfortunately, the Minimum Unit Price of Alcohol Bill did not receive full Executive support and will therefore not be introduced this mandate. Furthermore, the Bills pertaining to Mental Health and Capacity, Public Health, and Pharmacy Profession Regulation have been deferred until the next mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the current number of prisoners in Hydebank Wood College that have received a diagnosis of neurodivergence; and (ii) any support that is provided to help support these people.
answer_text: Based on the population at Hydebank Wood College on 27th March 2026, those with a confirmed diagnosis are ADHD 9; ASD 4; and Learning Disability 7.Residents with a diagnosis, will be referred into the appropriate pathway of support which includes Occupational Therapy, Psychology, Speech and Language Therapy, Special Educational Needs Teacher, Learning Disability Team, and Psychiatry, if a joint mental health condition, with key worker support.  Mental Health and Primary care teams also provide nursing support. A Prisoner Safety and Support Team works alongside residential teams and in collaboration with all departments to achieve the best outcomes for residents.  They work on a one-to-one basis providing additional support which can include fidget spinners, weighted blankets, ear defenders and distraction packs.  There are also a number of staff trained in Makaton.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Housing Executive will work with homeless groups to provide emergency crash beds for people who are sleeping on our streets, particularly during cold and severe weather conditions.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“There are 44 crash beds available to the Housing Executive on an ongoing basis. This has increased by 10 in the last 12 months.  As part of SWEP arrangements, the Housing Executive works with a range of providers to source additional emergency crash provision which can range from additional short-term provision within hostels or rest centres which act as a congregate crash facility. While availability can vary, this generally results in circa 20 additional beds being available when a SWEP is implemented. In respect of direct engagement with rough sleepers our focus will be on engaging with those organisations directly funded by the Housing Executive where the Housing Executive is responsible for ensuring that appropriate safeguarding procedures are in place via applicable contract management arrangements.”

The PHA funds two of the six Intensive Support Workers via Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s Drug Outreach Team and Extern’s Street Injectors Support Service.the PHA have supported the Housing Executive in developing The Women’s Hub to develop pathways to support for women experiencing Chronic Homelessness.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how his Department, in partnership with the Housing Executive and Housing Associations, plans to deliver additional supported living housing units in North Belfast.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive has recently completed a public consultation on the Supporting People (SP) Strategic Intent 2026–2031, which closed on 26 January 2026. The consultation sought views to inform the future direction of the SP Programme over the next five years and to shape associated operational delivery plans. In parallel, the Housing Executive has commenced a programme of work to modernise the SP Programme. A key element of the SP Modernisation Project 2025–2027 is the development of a Strategic Needs Assessment (SNA), which will provide a robust evidence base of current and projected housing-related support needs. The SNA will provide an evidence base at both local and thematic levels covering Homelessness, Disability & Mental Health, Young People and Older People.  The findings will inform future SP Programme priorities, including the consideration of gaps in provision and the potential reprofiling of services to better meet identified needs. There are two Supported Housing proposals currently included in the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) for North Belfast:Remodelling of Choice Housing-owned accommodation at 21 Skegoneill Avenue, Belfast to provide 4 self-contained flats. This proposal is included in the 2025/26 SHDP but is expected to slip to 2026/27. Remodelling of Choice Housing-owned property at Dumbarton House (79 Somerton Road), Belfast to provide accommodation for 13 clients plus staff. This proposal is included in the 2026/27 SHDP.New Supported Housing proposals are subject to capital and revenue funding. For both schemes above, associated business cases have been formally endorsed for SP funding (revenue), but Choice Housing have not yet submitted Applications for Project Approval (Housing Association Grant capital funding) to the Housing Executive’s Development Programme Group (DPG).”

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any support available to LGBTQIA+ pupils within schools; and (ii) any guidance provided to staff to support both pupils and staff in relation to LGBTQIA+ issues.
answer_text: Boards of Governors of grant-aided schools have statutory duties under Articles 17 and 18 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 to promote and safeguard the welfare of all of their pupils. Schools will have pastoral care policies and procedures in place to support pupils within the context of their setting. The REACH programme is also available across both primary and post-primary settings to offer support to pupils, and also engage with teachers and families, on matters that may be impacting the pupil’s emotional health and wellbeing or school experience.  Schools can access additional support and advice, to support pupil wellbeing through the Education Authority school support services, for example the Equality Team, Child Protection Support Service and the Addressing Bullying in Schools Team.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans there are for the Harberton North provision to be made permanent in North Belfast.
answer_text: On 13 October 2023 Development Proposal (DP) 702 was approved for Harberton School to change its designation from a 3-11 special school and to operate as a 3-19 special school across a dual campus with effect from 1 September 2023, or as soon as possible thereafter. The 3-19 school will provide for up to 500 special school places on a phased basis over both campuses - South (Harberton Park) and North (Fortwilliam). Initial refurbishment works were progressed on the Harberton North campus in 2021 which was followed by further works in 2022 and 2023 to expand the provision on the site.  In line with DP 702, the Special Schools – Plans of Action which published on 16 September, outline that the plan for Harberton Special School includes the continued development of the Harberton North site to provide accommodation for 320 pupils.  A business case which proposes a new build extension and a series of refurbishment and demolition works to provide additional teaching accommodation and address deficiencies in ancillary and support space is currently going through the approvals process.  Subject to the approval of this business case, and the receipt of the relevant statutory approvals, it is anticipated that the works will be complete in August 2031.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans there are to further develop appropriate tools for assessing special educational needs in Irish-medium settings.
answer_text: I recognise both the continued growth of the Irish-medium education sector and the significant contribution made by teachers, school leaders, advocacy bodies and parents in supporting that growth.Alongside this, I am equally aware of the challenges that have been repeatedly highlighted by the sector. My officials have been engaging extensively with stakeholders to identify the key issues facing Irish-medium schools, including those relating to special educational needs (SEN) provision and assessment.I have approved the development of a dedicated Irish-Medium Education Strategy and officials are progressing this work. The Strategy and accompanying Action Plan will seek to address the longstanding structural and operational barriers identified by the sector. Development is ongoing and publication is expected in 2027.I am aware of the particular concerns raised about the suitability of existing SEN assessment tools in Irish-medium settings, and of the impact this can have on timely and accurate identification of need. Work in this specific area is ongoing, and it is an identified priority within the early development phase of the IME Strategy.As part of the Strategy, officials in my Department will work closely with SEN policy colleagues, the Education Authority, assessment experts and Irish-medium stakeholders to explore the development or adaptation of appropriate bilingual assessment tools. This collaborative work will ensure that pupils in Irish-medium settings receive assessments that are linguistically appropriate, while maintaining the same standards applied across the education system. The aim will be to produce a practical and deliverable plan that supports better identification of need and promotes equitable access to SEN support for children learning through the medium of Irish.My department continues to promote best practice in the assessment of bilingual pupils and supports the use of evidence-based tools, combined with practitioner expertise, to ensure early identification and timely intervention for pupils in the Irish medium sector.I remain committed to ensuring that the needs of all pupils in Irish-medium education are recognised and supported, and that the development of the Irish-Medium Education Strategy reflects the voices, expertise and experiences of the sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on plans to provide mental health crisis de-escalation services, in North Belfast.
answer_text: There are a range of mental health crisis de-escalation services currently available in North Belfast. The regional Lifeline Helpline (0808 808 8000) is available 24 hours a day, seven days a week for anyone in distress or despair. Lifeline is available for anyone facing a crisis, feeling overwhelmed, or who just needs to talk to someone. When someone calls Lifeline, they will speak to a qualified counsellor. The counsellor will listen without judgement and work with the caller to help them stay safe from acting on thoughts of suicide.In addition, Lifeline can provide additional telephone support including safety check in calls; follow up calls and support calls. Lifeline also offers short term crisis counselling if required.Trust service for mental health crisis de-escalation in North Belfast is provided by the Mental Health Liaison Service, based at Fairview building, Mater Hospital. The Mental Health Liaison Service team cover both the Mater Hospital and Royal Victoria Hospital sites and assess patients who present in the Emergency Departments in mental health crisis and with serious mental illness. Mental Health Liaison will signpost, advise or implement an appropriate plan for the patient following assessment. Within the Royal Victoria Hospital and Mater Hospital sites additional support to those in mental health need within the Emergency Department is provided by the Community Navigators which is a community and voluntary sector service. Individuals can be referred to other community resources to support resolution of issues.The Mater site has opened the (Patient Assessment and Treatment Hub) PATH for individuals in mental health crisis to be supported in an appropriate environment away from ED.In line with the usual process for commissioning of services, any decision to commission further mental health crisis de-escalation services will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability. The needs of those in North Belfast would be considered alongside those of other areas in the region.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the rational for introducing changes to the Social Housing Development Grant to Housing Associations, which will produce fewer housing units, in areas of highest demand.
answer_text: The changes I announced to Social Housing Development Programme funding are in line with the existing methodology and are to ensure that the significant capital spend on social housing starts is based on an up-to-date cost assessment of the costs and incomes of new social housing. I have set out to the Housing Association sector my expectation that they, like the public sector, will deliver within challenging financial circumstances and strive for efficiencies.My decisions will ensure value for money is achieved and will also make my budget allocation stretch further. The changes, including the slightly lower average grant rate per unit, should enable delivery of more homes with the funding available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any vesting powers he has considered using to secure land for housing and regeneration projects, in areas of multiple deprivation.
answer_text: The Department holds the legislative power under Article 87 of the Housing (Northern Ireland) Order 1981 to vest land for regeneration purposes and social need. Where housing need has been established in an area and it cannot be addressed because there are no suitable sites available, the NIHE may ask the Department to use its vesting powers to acquire land for social housing.  The Department will consider using vesting powers in these circumstances, but the NIHE’s board must be able to demonstrate very clearly that the land is needed and no other suitable sites are available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on whether his Department will publish a strategy for the arts sector.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail how the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy addresses access to affordable childcare for those most in need.
answer_text: The childcare element of Universal Credit (UC), including the percentage contribution provided, is a matter for the Department for Communities (DfC).  DfC advises that the reimbursement of up to 85% of the cost of childcare for eligible Universal Credit claimants is a policy maintained by the Department for Work and Pensions in Great Britain. The same policy is implemented in Northern Ireland as a parity measure.  Diverging from parity on social security issues has financial implications for Northern Ireland as the costs of any such divergence have to be met by the Northern Ireland Executive.  In April 2025, DfC, in parity with DWP, increased the maximum payable amount of the UC childcare element from £950.92 to £1,031.88 for one child and from £1,630.15 to £1,768.94 for two or more children. In September 2024, I launched the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) which introduced a 15% subsidy for working parents eligible for Tax- Free Childcare (TFC), initially with children not yet at primary school. From September 2025, the scheme expanded to include children up to age 11 (aged 16 for children with a disability). When combined with TFC, eligible families receive up to 32% savings on their childcare costs.  Since its introduction, it is estimated that the NICSS has saved eligible families over £15m, and over £30m when combined with TFC savings.   I have also secured an Executive commitment to provide all children in the year before the start Primary school with 22.5 hours funded pre-school provision per week.  From September 2025, an additional 2,400 funded pre-school places in 105 settings have transitioned to full-time pre-school provision, including a free school meal for eligible children.   This means that 50% of pre-school places are now full-time, with further work planned to transition a further 100 settings to full-time from September 2026.These measures represent real progress and good value with the funding awarded by the Executive to date.  I remain committed to setting out what more is possible in the context of the full Early Learning and Childcare Strategy which I will bring forward for Executive consideration and public consultation in the coming weeks.  However, implementation of the Strategy will be subject to Executive approval and agreement to release the additional funding required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any plans his Department has to support the increased number of post-primary pupils educated through the medium of Irish in Belfast.
answer_text: My Department recognises the continuing growth in demand for post-primary education through the medium of Irish in Belfast and remains committed to supporting the sector. The Department’s statutory duty to encourage and facilitate Irish-medium education underpins this work.In the immediate term, enrolment pressures at Coláiste Feirste are being managed through the temporary variation process, which enables additional places to be approved where justified. This mechanism ensures that pupils who wish to pursue their education through Irish can access provision while longer-term solutions are developed.Under Operational Plan 2 of the ‘Strategic Area Plan for Sustainable Provision’ Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CnaG) is engaged in a (medium-term) workstream to bring forward a proposal for new post-primary Irish-medium provision to meet projected demand in Belfast and the surrounding area.In parallel, the Department is actively developing a new strategy for Irish-medium education. This will provide a framework to support the sector’s growth and sustainability into the future, ensuring that provision is well planned, high quality, and responsive to the needs of pupils, parents and communities.Taken together, these actions will help ensure that Irish-medium post-primary education in Belfast (and all areas) not only meets present pressures but is positioned to succeed in the years ahead.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the Baby Loss Certificate Scheme public consultation.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail why a privately-accessed ADHD diagnosis meets the criteria for Personal Independence Payment but does not meet the criteria for access to ADHD medication.
answer_text: Responsibility for the criteria for Personal Independence Payments sits with the Minister for Communities.The issue of access to ADHD medication relates to clinical responsibility and patient safety.  The legacy Health and Social Care Board issued correspondence on a number of occasions to private providers and general practice, most recently in June 2021, regarding requests for HSC prescriptions following private consultation.  The purpose of this correspondence was to remind both sectors of their clinical responsibilities in relation to prescribing of medications, including Red-Amber list drugs (which includes ADHD medication), to patients under their care and in particular, limitations with regards to requests to GPs for transfer of ongoing prescribing of medication, which for the patient’s GP may not be clinically acceptable.  This guidance has not changed.         To further facilitate appropriate shared care arrangements, the Department issued ‘Private (non-HSC) Prescribing Requests Guidance for Primary Care’ in September 2024. This guidance provides a reminder that the legal responsibility for prescribing lies with the prescriber who signs the prescription, and it is the responsibility of the individual prescriber to prescribe within their own level of competence and scope of practice. Whilst there is no obligation on GPs to prescribe, in instances where a GP considers that the medication being requested is clinically appropriate, falls within their area of competence, does not breach the terms of their contract or indemnity cover, and is in keeping with the General Medical Council (GMC) guidance on Good Medical Practice, then they may choose to prescribe. However, as clinical and legal liability associated with a prescribed medication falls to the doctor who issues and signs the script, ultimately a GP must be confident in the diagnosis, support and guidance given to them and the patient, by the recommending specialist.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the (i) funding awarded through Sport NI for boxing; (ii) the funds awarded to the High Performance Unit; and (iii) the clubs and individuals who received funding for boxing, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for Sport NI, I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has provided me with a response.Due to the volume of information to be provided I have arranged for the detail to be placed in the Assembly Library.I can also advise that all funding information is available on the Government Funding Database. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will be ceasing funding to Mermaids NI.
answer_text: My department does not provide funding to Mermaids NI. However, the Public Health Agency, has provided funding to Mermaids NI annually since 2022.  Prior to that funding was provided to the predecessor organisation SAIL.  There is a current contract in place which is continuing to be monitored through the normal contract management arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when her Department will install suicide prevention measures on bridges at the Westlink and Glengormley.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of developing suicide prevention measures at high risk locations on our infrastructure.I recently met with the Mental Health Champion, Professor Siobhan O’Neill to discuss the issue and the work my department can do to make a positive contribution to change.My Department has designed and tested a barrier system to prevent access at Clifton and Divis Street Bridges, as part of a pilot project and officials are currently finalising the detailed design, contract documents and business case for this scheme at an estimated investment of £1.8m.Due to significant funding pressures within my department it is likely to be 2026 before this work can be taken forward, however I will continue to bid for the necessary resources for this critical project, and would seek the support of my Executive colleagues in this endeavour.Following the implementation of the pilot scheme on 2 bridges over Westlink, we will undertake an assessment of its effectiveness and a further extension of the project may be taken forward, at other related areas such as the M2 bridges.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline why he has yet to deliver an oral statement to the Assembly on the concerns regarding the Cardiac Unit in the Royal Victoria Hospital and the measures he has placed on the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Whilst I have not delivered an oral statement, may I refer you to my written statement of 5 June, in which I outlined my concerns and measures regarding the findings of the Independent Review into the Cardiac Surgery Service in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust. I have held meetings with the Trust’s Chair, Trade Unions and professional bodies. May I further refer you to the Health Committee meeting on 12 June when the Committee was advised that a decision has been taken to increase the Trust’s escalation level to 5 as part of the measures, in accordance with the Support and Intervention Framework. I can confirm that my engagement with the Trust, Trade Unions and professional bodies will continue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail a breakdown of how many disabled recipients of Personal Independence Payment are likely be impacted by the cuts proposed by the Department for Work and Pensions.
answer_text: During the second reading of the Universal Credit and Personal Independence Payment Bill in Parliament, the UK Government announced a significant change to its welfare plans, stating that any future reform of the eligibility conditions for Personal Independence Payment will now be subject to the outcome of a review in relation to the assessment criteria.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail: (i) the amount of funding has been spent on staff from the Strategic Investment Board as part of the development of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategy; and (ii) when that funding started and ended within the Executive Office.
answer_text: From the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls (EVAWG) Directorate’s inception in December 2021 until 31 March 2025, £811,367 has been spent on Strategic Investment Board (SIB) costs.  This is broken down as follows:2021 – 2022: £5,2002022 – 2023: £244,3092023 – 2024: £207,9682024 – 2025: £353,890SIB was asked to support the EVAWG programme to deliver aspects of the work where appropriately skilled staff could not be sourced due to staffing shortages across the NICS, or where specialist skills were required. These specialist skills were in the fields of communication, campaign and brand development, and data analytics.While there will be some SIB costs during 2025/ 26, these will be greatly reduced in comparison to the previous year. The EVAWG team’s move into delivery phase has provided an opportunity to review the staffing structure and the support arrangements needed to effectively deliver future work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail a breakdown of how many Housing Associations have been able to avail of Financial Transactions Capital funding to build social housing.
answer_text: Financial Transactions Capital (FTC) funding is not used by Housing Associations in the delivery of new social homes. This form of funding would be required to be repaid by Housing Associations and would impact on their gearing ratio and therefore their ability to gain external loans. FTC loans are not a substitute for market loans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department is considering security measures for those families from Annalee Street and Alloa Street, North Belfast, who had their homes attacked and their lives threatened.
answer_text: I utterly condemn the recent sectarian attacks in north Belfast. Everyone deserves to live without fear and to feel safe in their own home, regardless of background or religion and I call on those responsible for intimidating and attacking families and causing fear within communities to stop.Families are being supported through referrals to the Hate Crime Advocacy Service and Hate Incident Practical Action scheme, both part funded by my Department, to ensure that they are provided with the necessary support and additional security measures that may assist in keeping them safe whilst the investigation remains ongoing.Referrals have also been made to the Home Secure Scheme, funded by Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs) which can provide a range of security devices, including door alarms, window alarms, and other security equipment, based on a home safety assessment.My Department is also working to introduce strengthened hate crime legislation in the current mandate. This legislation will establish a statutory aggravation model for hate crimes and provide enhanced protection for victims of hate crime.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he would consider removing the inclusion of adoption status from a birth certificate for anyone who was adopted, when they are seeking a copy of their birth certificate.
answer_text: ANSWERThe marking of adopted status on a birth certificate is a requirement in primary legislation under the Adoption (Northern Ireland) Order 1987. Although there is no adoption marking on a short birth certificate, a marking does appear on the long format certificate. I recognise having to produce a long birth certificate that indicates that a person is adopted could feel disclosing for some. Primary legislation would be needed to effect a change to this position.My officials are currently progressing the Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill, which will make permanent the temporary provisions in the Coronavirus Act 2020 for the registration of deaths and still-births and will provide an enabling power for the establishment of a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme.   They will return to this matter once that Bill has been progressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline whether the Housing Executive will award intimidation points to the families who had their homes attacked in Annalee and Alloa Streets in North Belfast.
answer_text: Rule 23 was removed from the Housing Selection Scheme with effect from 1 April 2025, following which there will be no further awards of Intimidation Points. The relevant housing and statutory bodies are working with those impacted by these attacks, and will continue to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on engagement with the British Government on the Spending Review 2025.
To ask the Minister of Health when he will bring forward Safe and Effective Staffing legislation.
answer_text: Work is progressing to bring forward this legislation. Following extensive engagement with Trade Unions, HSC Trust representatives and other stakeholders, a public consultation ran from 22nd July to 14th October last year.Officials in my Department are currently analysing the responses with a view to preparing a Government Response for publication within the coming weeks. My Department is also continuing to engage with stakeholders to refine and finalise the policy position before seeking agreement to instruct the Office of the Legislative Counsel (OLC) to begin drafting the bill. I would optimistically hope, contingent on Executive agreement and OLC priorities, that drafting of the Bill and eventual introduction to the Assembly will happen before the end of the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the community groups that his Department is funding childcare places in, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My Department does not directly fund childcare places however it does support community and voluntary groups to provide a range early learning and childcare provision in local areas.  Details of the different schemes have been deposited in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to confirm when he will bring forward the Sexual Orientation Strategy.
answer_text: Following consideration of the work previously carried out to develop the Social Inclusion Strategies, alongside unprecedented pressures on resources across Executive departments, I decided to move forward on development of the Strategies on a phased basis, with work now well progressed, on the Anti-Poverty and Disability Strategies. Further detail on the timetable for the development of the Sexual Orientation Strategy will be confirmed shortly.Final decisions on content, the timing of consultation and publication of the Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide details on all Multi-Disciplinary Teams based in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Primary Care Multidisciplinary Team (MDT) Programme is a partnership between GP Federations and HSC Trusts which introduces new physiotherapy, social work and mental health roles into general practice, to work alongside the existing practice teams.West Belfast Federation is currently the only federation in the BHSCT that has an MDT footprint, and at 31 December 2024, had the following MDT staff in post.First Contact Physiotherapist (FCP) 9.7 WTE (Includes FCP Manager)Social Work (SW)                           11.0 WTE (Includes SW Manager)Social Work Assistant (SWA)          5.0  WTESenior Mental Health Practitioner    11.1 WTE (Includes MHP Manager)The MDT Programme remains key to stabilising and strengthening vital Primary Care services and the confirmation of £61m of Transformation funding will see the MDT Programme brought to completion in the 7 GP Federations which have already commenced and expansion into an additional 5 GP Federation areas with a combined population of 670,000. This would see MDTs complete in West Belfast and expand into North Belfast.Detailed plans are currently being developed between Trusts and GP Federations at a local level to determine the sequencing and timescale of investment in practices.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of children in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust waiting to have their tonsils removed; and (ii) how long the waiting list is for this procedure.
answer_text: The Belfast Health Social Care Trust continues to focus on reducing waiting times for children waiting to have their tonsils removed.  Despite this, however, the total number of children waiting tonsillectomy is 419 as at the start of April 2025.(i)	The total number of children awaiting tonsillectomy in Royal Victoria Hospital (RVH) is 218(ii)	There is currently a 2-year waiting time but this is steadily reducing and should be reduced to 1 year by summer 2025.For those children, with more complex needs, who require surgery in the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children (RBHSC), there is a much longer waiting time due to the limitations of available beds and Ears Nose and Throat (ENT) theatre lists in the hospital(i)	The total number of children awaiting tonsillectomy in RBHSC is 201 (ii)	and there is currently a 5-year waiting timeOfficials have engaged with the Belfast HSC Trust to explore ways of reducing waiting times for children awaiting tonsillectomy surgery in ENT.  Unfortunately, however, as already mentioned, the Trust has limited scope to increase theatre capacity and there is a limited number of beds available for what are often complex cases involving an overnight stay.  Working within current resources, however, the Trust regularly validates waiting lists to ensure that children still require the surgery or if they can be transferred from RBHSC to the main Royal Victoria Hospital.The service also continues to avail of additional funding to reduce the waiting list for paediatric tonsillectomy surgery when available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how much of the £215 million required to tackle surgical waiting lists will be used within health and social care.
answer_text: As outlined in my letter to MLAs on 6 April 2025, while the waiting list monies included in the 2025-26 budget add up to £215m, they do not meet in full the targeted £215m investment set out in the Programme for Government. The bulk of the investment announced for waiting lists and elective care in the 2025-26 budget is not new or additional funding. All but £50m of it has been ringfenced from within Health’s existing allocation. Ringfencing some £165m from within this budget, rather than providing it as an additional allocation, as per the initial spirit of the Programme for Government commitment, will exacerbate the shortfall. This will intensify already severe pressures on HSC Trusts to make further savings and efficiencies and live within their means. In light of the additional demands now placed on the HSC system by the confirmed NI budget, I will be engaging with system leaders on next steps and how best to apply the ringfenced funding to help tackle waiting lists.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of children and young people awaiting a first appointment with the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of children and young people in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust awaiting a neurodivergent diagnosis.
answer_text: Neurodivergent conditions include Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD), Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), Dyspraxia, Dyslexia, Dysgraphia, Dyscalculia and Tourette’s Syndrome (TS) as well as many other conditions. HSC Trusts do not regularly provide waiting list reports on all of these conditions. Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has gone live on Encompass and their data is undergoing extensive validation. There is no validated reporting available from the Trust at this time.My Department is planning to launch a public consultation on the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework in May which will guide future development of services. The Framework embraces a move towards supporting ASD and ADHD services within a broader inclusive neurodevelopmental approach that will incorporate regionally consistent single points of referral and diagnostic assessment pathways.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he is considering legislation to give the Housing Executive land assembly powers.
answer_text: I recently launched a 5-year pilot with the Housing Executive and local council planning officials. The aim of the pilot, called Housing Executive Land Acquisition (HELA), is to identify sufficient land to deliver 100 social housing units in areas of acute housing need.I recognise that availability of suitable land is one of the key issues impacting on housing supply more widely and I have spoken in the Assembly recently, about my determination to identify and release public land to provide new housing. I also stated my intention to bring a paper to my Executive colleagues in due course, to set out new proposals for disposal of public land. I am also pleased to support the work of DoF Land & Property Services which has established a new division, called the Strategic Asset Management Unit (SAMU), from 1st April 2025. This new division is a professional support service for departments and their Arm’s Length Bodies offering strategic advice, planning and reporting to better inform asset management decisions, including supporting the identification of publicly owned land for housing within the remit of the Government Land and Property Register.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Mental Health Navigators services at the Emergency Department in (i) the Mater Hospital; and (iI) the Royal Hospitals will be fully funded for the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: The Community Navigator Service is currently without recurrent funding as it was initially funded as a pilot in one Trust area. Following the two-year pilot period, SPPG in the Department provided non-recurrent funding through slippage and I understand an extension to the original pilot has been funded directly by the Belfast Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust.In the absence of additional funding for mental health, regrettably, there is no recurrent funding which can be provided by the Department. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he has taken to ensure that lesbian and bisexual women have access to fertility treatment.
answer_text: Publicly funded fertility treatment in Northern Ireland is available to all women who meet the criteria, including lesbian and bisexual women.The criteria can be found on the Belfast Trust’s website at Eligibility-for-HSC-funded-IVF-and-related-treatments-effective-from-1st-June-2019 (15).pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide details on a health and social care strategy for women.
answer_text: In view of my constrained budget, I intend to focus during the remainder of this mandate on the actions that can be delivered within current resources to stabilise our Health and Social Care system and address the health inequalities that contribute to poor health outcomes, rather than commit to any new long-term strategies which cannot be funded at this time. Work to develop a Women’s Health Action Plan is ongoing, which will include the wide range of policy and service development initiatives currently in progress and outline any actions that can be delivered within current resources over the next three years to improve health services and deliver better outcomes for women.  My Department recently hosted a number of online engagement events with women's representative groups and healthcare professionals and will be reviewing the input received over the coming weeks to help inform the priorities in the action plan.  My Department also provided funding to Derry Well Women, who in partnership with Queen’s University Belfast, developed a Northern Ireland Women’s Health Survey as Phase 1 of a large-scale public listening exercise with women.  I launched this survey in November 2024 with the aim of hearing directly from women about their health priorities and barriers to accessing services.  The survey closed on 31 March 2025.  The Department will consider the early findings from this exercise as work develops on the Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the support available to children and young People in schools in relation to anti-bullying, with a particular focus on LGBTQIA+ children and young people.
answer_text: The Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (the Act), requires schools to record incidents of bullying type behaviour, their motivation, methods used and their outcome.  The legislation provides guidance on exemplar motivations to inform the school assessment and recording process. This list reflects Section 75 groups including those who may be targeted due to their sexual orientation, gender, gender identity and perceived gender identity.Capturing potential motivation for reported bullying type behaviour is important to enable identification of school specific issues, patterns and trends, such as homophobic bullying type behaviour. In this data informed context, staff are better placed to address need appropriately with tailored pastoral, safeguarding and curricular interventions.The Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT) within the Education Authority provide support for schools, educational settings, young people and parents/carers to help them understand the requirements of the Act. ABSIT focuses on supporting the development of inclusive school based systemic approach to empower staff in effective prevention and response to bullying type behaviour. The team provide bespoke support with complex incidents of bullying type behaviour to aid inclusion. ABSIT are members of the Safeguarding Board Northern Ireland (SBNI) and Anti Bullying Alliance which are umbrella organisations comprising broad representation from third sector bodies, many of whom address the needs of Section 75 communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the sectoral study on the Cost of Doing Business in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when the 15-year old MRI scanner in the Mater Hospital will be replaced; and (ii) what alternative arrangements there are within the Mater Hospital to provide a scanner for instances when it breaks down.
answer_text: The MRI scanner in the Mater Hospital was purchased through Charitable Funds in 2006. The MRI scanner is 18 years old. Efforts have been made to replace the scanner in its current location, however, this could not be achieved whilst being compliant with current building regulations and best practice for a modern MRI department. The Belfast HSC Trust has identified a separate area on the ground floor of the Mater Hospital which may be suitable to develop a modern MRI department with potential for expansion space for a second MRI scanner. The Belfast HSC Trust has commissioned a feasibility study which is now nearing completion. This will inform the design and outline costs for the project which can then be considered by the Trust’s Capital Evaluation Team and the Imaging Service. It is anticipated that the feasibility study will conclude in the coming months. The timelines of replacement will then be dependent on capital funding being made available for this important project. When the Mater MRI scanner breaks down, the contingency arrangement is for patients to be conveyed to another Belfast HSC Trust site for their MRI scan. Cardiac MRI scanning can be accommodated on the Belfast City Hospital site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount of money from public funds was invested into the private sector for radiotherapy services in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust from February 2024 to February 2025.
answer_text: There was no money from public funds invested into the private sector for radiotherapy services in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust from February 2024 to February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the mental health crisis de-escalation services in North Belfast.
answer_text: The statutory service for mental health crisis de-escalation in North Belfast is provided by the Mental Health Liaison Service, based at Fairview building, Mater Hospital. The Mental Health Liaison Service team cover both the Mater Hospital and Royal Victoria Hospital sites and assess patients who present in the Emergency Departments in mental health crisis and with serious mental illness. Based on the outcome of the assessment Mental Health Liaison will signpost, advise or implement an appropriate plan for the patient.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline a timeline for his plans to introduce a lung cancer screening programme.
answer_text: The former Minister of Health, Minister Swann, accepted the recommendation by the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC) to introduce a targeted lung cancer screening programme for asymptomatic people with a history of smoking, aged between 55-74 years old, in tandem with the enhanced integration of smoking cessation services.We are currently scoping out what the requirements of this new programme for Northern Ireland will be.  This will be a very significant service development encompassing new screening services and a range of additional diagnostic and treatment services. This work will include expansion of the workforce and infrastructure requiring input and collaboration from multiple partners. This requires time and significant financial resources.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide reassurances to the victims and survivors of Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses that the context of patriarchy will be referenced throughout any final report.
answer_text: It would not be right or appropriate for the Department to outline what should be in an independent investigation’s report, but it is clear the scope of the integrated investigation includes examining gender-based harms.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide details on their engagement with the Truth Recovery Panel and victims and survivors, through the lens of gender.
answer_text: We met with the co-chairs of the Panel in November and were pleased to note the significant progress made to date.As the Truth Recovery Design Panel report made clear, the investigation should examine the experience of women and girls in the institutions and is fundamentally about gender-based harms.The work of the Truth Recovery Programme is therefore underscored by consideration of discrimination against women and girls and viewed through a gender prism.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) whether departmental officials have made them aware of ongoing complaints from victims and survivors in relation to a perceived lack of sensitivity to specific gender concerns and a reluctance to highlight the context of misogyny from the Truth Recovery Panel; and (ii) whether they will ensure that officials provide them with an update on such complaints and any potential resolution.
answer_text: Departmental officials are aware of concerns which were considered by the Panel. Any complaints received in respect of the Independent Panel are managed in line with the Panel’s complaints policy, which is on the Panel's website. If a complaint is received, it is taken seriously with clear escalation processes in place. It is important complaints are considered in line with the policy, but we have asked officials to keep us informed if complaints are escalated to the Department.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any actions taken by the Truth Recovery Panel to build trust within the Victims and Survivors Forum since some of their complaints were raised with the Executive Office on 29 January 2025.
answer_text: The Truth Recovery Independent Panel is independent of the Department. Information on these issues is best sought from the Panel. We also understand that the Panel has contacted Committee members seeking to establish the detail of the concerns raised on 29 January 2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the draft Refugee Integration Strategy.
answer_text: We are pleased to have approved the draft Refugee Integration Strategy and associated Delivery Framework and obtained Executive approval.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how state agencies, as well as religious orders, will be made accountable for the pathways, forced adoption and abuse of women and girls within their care.
answer_text: The first stage of the Truth Recovery integrated investigation is the non-statutory investigation, which is being led by the Independent Panel. Within its investigation and as part of its terms of reference, the Independent Panel will examine: ‘how violations were inflicted and persisted’; ‘the institutional and societal causes of the violations’; and ‘the responses of involved institutions, organisations and agencies to individuals and families affected’.  The second stage of the investigation is a statutory Public Inquiry, to which the Independent Panel will make recommendations.  These will include measures necessary to respond to, and prevention of recurrence of harms identified.  Building on the Panel’s work, the Public Inquiry will seek to answer 3 core questions: what happened, why it happened, and who was responsible.  The latter question will consider persons and agencies who may have been involved, including institutions and the state, to determine any systemic failings.  In parallel with the Public Inquiry and subject to the passing of legislation, a state redress scheme will be established which will provide financial redress to affected individuals.  We are committed to seeking appropriate contributions from those responsible following the Public Inquiry: that could include organisations, institutions, and state, including from the Treasury.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any measures her Department is taking to support co-operatives.
answer_text: Question 8 AQO 1592/22-27 grouped with Question 13 AQO 1597/22-27My Department continues to make positive progress around Community Wealth Building and the recommendations in the 2022 report.  Last week, I met with the Ministers for Finance and Communities to discuss our ongoing progress in advance of meeting with Development Trusts NI.I was delighted to attend a meeting to learn about the work the Larne Community Wealth Building initiative has undertaken, and to hear directly from members about their future plans. I was encouraged by the collaborative approach, not only between the anchor organisations, but also the shared learning between Larne and the North West Community Wealth Building Partnerships.One of the recommendations from the 2022 report asked the department to “explore the potential for cooperatives, employee ownership, and worker buyouts.”In August my department published research on the benefits, and potential growth, of employee ownership models. Employee Ownership Ireland was recently established to support businesses exploring employee ownership as a future business model. I will be meeting representatives from this organisation shortly to discuss how we can support this work further.  I attended the “Co-operation among Co-operators” conference last week, held in Boundary Brewery, a local co-operative. I spoke about the important role these business models can play in delivering my economic priorities. I was pleased that my department could provide bursaries to ensure the conference was accessible for everyone, and I am excited to see where the conversations that began there will lead. Community energy is a sector where there is potential for a cooperative-type model. DfE officials are part of the Sustainable Energy Communities project advisory panel, led by Advice NI. My predecessor commissioned the Centre of Advanced Sustainable Energy (CASE) to undertake a Community Decarbonisation Pathfinder Project. This work is in the early stages of development with the key stakeholders meeting monthly to develop the project.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on funding provided by their Department to promote good relations in North Belfast.
answer_text: We have invested significantly in North Belfast, with over £1.2million of Good Relations funding in the area in 2024/25. This includes 11 T:BUC Camps, 21 Planned Intervention Projects, 22 projects through the District Council Good Relations Programme, and nine Central Good Relations projects. The North Belfast Strategic Good Relations Programme is supporting delivery of 40 projects. Seven projects are being funded through CRC’s small grant scheme and six organisations are receiving CRC Core Funding. This is in addition to the Urban Village investment of over £2million since 2018 and the PEACEPLUS council programme which will see £1.4million invested in the coming years.We know our investment is having a positive impact.  Across North Belfast, the number of Sectarian Crimes reported has decreased between 2005/2006 and 2023/2024, demonstrating the collective impact of our interventions. 		  And in the last year more than four fifths of participants on the North Belfast Good Relations Programme reported improved cross-community relationships. We look forward to developing a refreshed approach to this work as we consider the findings of the T:BUC Review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the tools used in assessing special educational needs in the Irish-medium sector.
answer_text: Special educational needs (SEN) may be identified by educational staff in schools or by Education Authority (EA) staff.Schools may use teacher-made assessment tools for informal use or avail of commercial tools which are available to them, provided they have the appropriate qualification to do so.When a child is accepted for statutory assessment, they will be assessed by an Educational Psychologist (EP) from the EA.  They use a range of methods and tools including consultation to support schools and parents, questionnaires and Talking Mats.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the amount of funding currently provided to the private sector to deliver health and social care; and (ii) the services being contracted by his Department from the private health and social care sector.
answer_text: The amount of funding currently provided to the private sector to deliver health and social care services is summarised below by Trust: TrustHealth Care (£m)Social Care (£m) BHSCT13.2309.7NHSCT10.0290.9SEHSCT13.8182.6SHSCT11.0197.0WHSCT9.4210.3Total57.41,190.5The individual services being contracted from the private health and social care sector vary by Trust. Within health care expenditure, services generally include outpatient, day case, inpatient diagnostic and surgical activity. This will include private sector elective access relating to Waiting List Initiatives. Within social care expenditure primarily relates to services such as nursing and residential care home placements, domiciliary care and supported living.Given the significant pressure on our health and social care system, the growing demand and capacity gap means that over recent years our waiting list activity has become increasingly important in dealing with Red Flag and time critical patients. To achieve the maximum impact, the system relies on use of the independent sector (private health and social care providers) to deliver additional waiting list activity and social care services.  With the scale of the backlog of patients waiting for hospital treatment and community care, the HSC capacity secured from independent sector providers has been crucial for the many people that have benefited from it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the strategies that his Department has introduced to reduce surgical waiting lists; and (ii) how this information is communicated to patients and their GPs.
answer_text: The updated Elective Care Framework (ECF), published in May 2024, provides the strategic plan for the development and reform of elective care services here over the next five years and details the measures needed to address the long waiting lists. However, delivering on that plan requires levels of investment which the current draft budget cannot provide. Although much more is needed, progress has already been made on the implementation of a number of actions outlined within the ECF, specifically to support the reduction of surgical waiting lists. Examples of key pieces of work include: continued development of elective care centres through regional day procedure centres (DPCs) and elective overnight stay centres (EOSCs) to maximise all regional capacitycontinued expansion of rapid diagnosis centres (RDCs) and the use of mega clinics to ensure patients are fit for surgery and pools of suitable patients are available; this will enable last minute cancellation slots to be filled and thereby ensure all available theatre capacity is maximisedcontinued programme of service reviews across a number of specialties to drive forward ongoing improvementscontinued efforts to increase efficiency and effectiveness through the use of best practice service improvement guidance including:increased day surgery rates using the British Association of Day Surgery (BADS) best practice guidance increased day of surgery admission as clinically appropriatemaximise theatre utilisationreduced ‘Did Not Attend’ (DNAs) / cancelled on the day (CNDs) ratesThese are not one-off initiatives, but continuing reform and efficiency work, all of which have contributed to small, but tangible, improvements to the waiting list position here. However, without the investment needed, it will be difficult to maintain momentum in reducing surgical waits.As these initiatives are rolled out across the region, the HSC employs a variety of communication methods including patient letters, leaflets, website information and other media channels.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of respite places for (i) children and young people with learning or physical disabilities; (ii) children and young people with mental health issues; (iii) adults with learning or physical disabilities; and (iv) adults with mental health issues, in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) are responsible for the planning and delivery of respite/short break services, aligned to assessed needs. The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust have provided the following response:Children and young people with learning or physical disabilities; Currently the Trust provides 4 respite beds; 2 beds are accessible to children with learning disabilities and 2 beds are accessible to children with physical disabilities.Children and young people with mental health issues;Currently Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services are not commissioned for and do not provide respite care for children and young people with mental health issues. Respite may however be provided by statutory Social Work Services through Family Intervention and Child Protection teams, if also involved. Adults with learning or physical disabilities;         Adult Learning Disability Services: Currently there are 6          respite commissioned beds, 15 statutory respite beds and 1          emergency respite bed for adults with learning disabilities.Physical and Sensory Disability Services: The Trust does not have any identified respite placements specifically for adults with physical disabilities. The Trust does however offer respite via other avenues such as care homes or neurology beds in accordance with assessed need.Adults with mental health issues; Adult Mental Health Services:        There are no dedicated respite beds for adults with mental health        issues. The Trust does however offer respite for example via care         home settings akin to other Adult Programmes of Care, such as         Physical Disability and Learning Disability Services.In addition to respite/ short breaks provided within statutory and independent sector care homes, within both Children’s and Adult Services across HSCTs respite/ short breaks are also provided within the person’s own home, through the provision of enhanced domiciliary care packages or within the homes of Foster Carers or Shared Lives Carers. I am aware of the importance of short break/respite provision in terms of supporting carers and families in their caring role. My Department is currently finalising the Children with Disability Framework and the Learning Disability Service Model for Northern Ireland. Both aim to respond to increased challenges faced by families of children and adults with disability and to support HSCTS in meeting rising demands and increased complexity of need. It is recognised that reform of how services are structured, delivered and resourced, both from a regional perspective and locally in each of the HSCTs is required, including the provision of respite/short break services. On 17 October 24, I approved the allocation of £2 million in year for children with disabilities, with a recurrent impact of £13.1 million. My Department has ensured this funding has been ringfenced to directly improve respite/short breaks capacity, while rapidly expanding a range of family, ther

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any plans her Department has in ensuring that family courts are more accessible for people trying to access non-molestation orders.
answer_text: There is legislative provision in place to assist people to access a non-molestation order.Under the Family Homes and Domestic Violence (NI) Order 1998 (for which the Department of Finance has policy responsibility) a court can make a non-molestation and/or occupation order without the respondent having been given notice of the proceedings.  These ex-parte applications are heard in chambers or closed court if they are not listed in a domestic or family proceedings court.  I brought forward provisions to enhance the protections available to victims of domestic abuse giving evidence in family cases. The new provisions, which apply to non-molestation order applications, include, for example, provision for special measures to enable evidence to be given from behind a screen, or outside the courtroom.Case management is a matter for the independent judiciary.There is no court fee for a non-molestation or occupation order application.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide an update on the sectoral study on the Cost of Doing Business in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I commissioned the Ulster University Economic Policy Centre in December to carry out an independent study into the cost of doing business here following the concerns raised in the aftermath of the Chancellors’ Autumn budget. In particular, employers understandably have significant concerns regarding the cost impacts of the planned changes in Employer National Insurance Contributions.  This is in addition to the other cost pressures they are already facing, such as insurance and energy. The Ulster University study will examine which sectors will be hit the hardest, and if the impacts are more severe locally given the size and structural differences in our sectors compared to Britain.It is important that we hear the voices of those most impacted which is why the Economic Policy Centre team will be engaging widely with business bodies, community and voluntary sectors as they undertake this research. A Terms of Reference has been published on my department’s website and a final report is expected to be delivered by the end of March 2025.
