question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the arrangements the Housing Executive and other statutory partners are putting in place to provide shelter and support for those who are sleeping on the streets.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive funds a rough sleeper outreach service in Belfast (delivered by The Welcome Organisation) and drop-in centres in Belfast and Derry/Londonderry (delivered by The Welcome Organisation and Foyle Haven respectively). These services are available throughout the year and provide a range of support to those who are rough sleeping in each city. Following the closure of the Welcome Drop-in at Townsend Street in Belfast an interim location has been secured from 3 December to provide a much-needed support hub, essential services, support connections, and a structured environment for individuals experiencing homelessness. Other options are being sought for a more permanent solution.Work is ongoing between the Housing Executive and a range of key partners to deliver a Severe Weather Emergency Protocol (SWEP). The SWEP provides extended support and accommodation for people experiencing rough sleeping but is only implemented when a high risk to life is considered, such as freezing conditions. SWEP has been implemented recently on 10 occasions from 18 November to 19 December.The delivery of the Complex Lives Model is also supported by the Housing Executive, taking a whole system approach to supporting people experiencing and exiting chronic homelessness.  This includes supporting people who are caught in the cycle of rough sleeping, substance misuse, poor physical and mental health and offending behaviour as well as those exiting chronic homelessness and beginning tenancies.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the measures his Department is taking with the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust Estate Management to ensure that public money is protected.
answer_text: DoH Health Estates is the formally accredited Department of Health Centre of Procurement Expertise (CoPE) for construction works and design services.In their role as CoPE, Health Estates have produced an Estates Procurement Manual (EPM) for use by the arm’s length bodies of which the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust is one.  The EPM outlines a series of procedures which must be followed by the bodies when managing the procurement and delivery of construction works, design services and construction maintenance.  Compliance with these procedures ensures adherence to relevant procurement law, best practice and Northern Ireland Public Procurement Policy all of which are designed to deliver value for money and that public money is protected.Compliance with the EPM is monitored by my Department on an annual basis with production of an Estate Procurement Performance Report.The Estates Procurement Manual can be accessed at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/estates-procurement-manual .

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans for mental health crisis de-escalation services in Belfast.
answer_text: Implementing the new regional crisis service is a commitment in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, under actions 12 and 27. The Department of Health Strategic Planning and Performance Group and the Public Health Agency are working in partnership to deliver on both of these actions and to develop a Regional Mental Health Crisis Service for all citizens of Northern Ireland. The components of the proposed regional mental health crisis service are: accessing mental health crisis services, primary care and interagency partnership, community crisis service, mental health liaison service and crisis resolution home treatment. A mapping report of current crisis services including community and voluntary sector is currently been finalised. Phase 2 future state work has commenced across lifespan services including scoping what a good service would look like. A Regional Mental Health Crisis Service workshop took place on the 11th October 2024 with relevant stakeholders across lifespan services. A draft Regional Mental Health Crisis Service Lifespan Pathway was presented. Stakeholders provided feedback on the draft pathway, discussed gaps within services and how best to take forward the next phase of work of the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service model. My Officials are currently reviewing the feedback in order to progress the Phase 2 future state work.In line with the usual process for commissioning of services, any decision to commission mental health crisis services which will include de-escalation services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability. The needs of those in Belfast would be considered alongside those of other areas in the region

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the role of the Strategic Investment Board in the design, development and delivery of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework.
answer_text: A range of experienced Strategic Investment Board (SIB) staff have worked on the EVAWG programme, to provide expertise across several key areas.  The integration of a full time Strategic Communications and Engagement Specialist assisted in establishing the EVAWG identity, fostering meaningful stakeholder engagement, and managing the successful co-design process. This includes delivering on the communications and campaigns elements of the Delivery Plan, raising awareness and providing information at a societal level, whilst ensuring that there is strong consistent messaging.			   Other temporary support has been provided to support the strategic communications and engagement work-strand, as well as the development of the Change Fund. 				Additionally, a part time Data Scientist has worked with the EVAWG team to develop a comprehensive and robust framework for measuring impact.EVAWG engaged SIB to provide specialist support in developing the suite of Outline Business Cases relating to preparatory works and to the first Delivery Plan. These documents have been crucial in establishing the programme’s foundation, with clear, robust and well supported evidence to support delivery.SIB also supported EVAWG on an ad-hoc basis including the development of the Equality Impact Assessment, a Policy Landscape Review, General Frontline Services Landscape Review, and the Evaluation of Belfast and Lisburn’s Women’s Aid Victims Support worker project. 			     The Small Business Research Initiative (SBRI) process is being managed on behalf of TEO by the Strategic Investment Board, who are the appointed Centre of Procurement Excellence for SBRIs here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide an overview of how vulnerable prisoners are cared for in custody.
answer_text: The care of people who come into contact with the Justice system is of paramount importance and is taken very seriously by my Department. This is particularly important in the context of the work of the Northern Ireland Prison Service, as we seek to care for and support people with complex and challenging needs who find themselves in prison custody. Healthcare services for people in prison custody in Northern Ireland are provided on behalf of the Department of Health by the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust. This includes primary healthcare, mental healthcare and addiction services.While responsibility for physical health, mental health and addiction services sits with the Department of Health, I recognise that everyone has a role to play in supporting people who have additional needs in the prison community. My Department is engaged with a number of initiatives that are aimed at improving mental health and reducing the harm for people in Northern Ireland and these include:My role within the Executive Working Group on Mental Wellbeing, Resilience and Suicide PreventionEngagement with the Mental Health Champion, whose role is jointly funded by all DepartmentsThe implementation of the 10 year Mental Health Strategy for Northern Ireland and the 10 year Strategic Framework to tackle the harm caused by Substance Use for Northern Ireland: Reducing Harm, Empowering RecoveryRepresentation on the Protect Life 2 Steering Group and Towards Zero Suicide Collaborative BoardRepresentation on the Making Life Better All Departments Officials GroupRepresentation on the Forensic Managed Care Network While the people living in prison in Northern Ireland are supported by good mental health and addictions healthcare, there is more to be done and through working together we will provide good outcomes for those in our care.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an overview on how he will work with NI Water to ensure that homes developed in areas of highest demand will be able to connect to water and sewage facilities.
answer_text: I recognise the current wastewater capacity challenges and am taking a three-pronged approach to this matter.  Firstly, I continue to work with my Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment for NI Water, secondly, I am bringing forward new legislation in relation to sustainable drainage systems (SuDs), which will help to store stormwater so that it releases slower into the wastewater network and thirdly, I have asked my officials to examine the legislative change required to bring forward developer contributions. I have provided NI Water with a total indicative budget allocation of almost £0.5billion for 2024/25, representing just under 40% of the total non-ring-fenced budget for my Department. This reflects the central importance of water and sewerage services to our society and, in particular, in addressing wastewater treatment issues. NI Water is a regulated utility, and it works with the Utility Regulator and environmental regulator to ensure that investment is targeted where it is most needed.However, I would encourage developers to engage early with NI Water.  I am aware that NI Water has established a Development Constraints Project Team, to help alleviate capacity constraints, and is encouraging early engagement with developers, through the Pre- Development Enquiry process, to arrange for initial discussions on wastewater issues at the beginning of the process to try and find a resolution to any issues at an early stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the support provided to prisoners who come into custody with an autism spectrum disorder.
answer_text: Responsibility for health and social care of prisoners in Northern Ireland sits with the Department of Health who have commissioned South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (the Trust) to provide primary, mental health and addictions services within prisons. Many people arrive in prison with complex needs. Support is available for people who have additional needs, including those who have been confirmed by the Trust as having an autism spectrum disorder.  This includes access to learning disability nurses and speech and language therapists, together with environmental changes, access to garden spaces and therapy animals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Mater Hospital will have a full operating theatre, including an intensive care unit, reinstated following the Covid pandemic.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has confirmed that, following the Covid pandemic, the Mater operating theatres are fully operational, providing non-complex intermediate surgery.The unit and the operating theatres in the Mater Hospital provide a service for intermediate surgery for which there is no requirement for an Intensive Care Unit. Intensive Care Services for Belfast Health and Social Care Trust are provided across the Royal Victoria Hospital and Belfast City Hospital sites. Following the pandemic and publication of the Elective Care Framework, Belfast City Hospital was designated as Northern Ireland’s main elective site for complex surgery. Intensive Care Services were increased in Belfast City Hospital to support the change for complex elective surgery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any action her Department is taking to contribute towards the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls.
answer_text: The interdependencies between the new Justice and Health-led Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy and the Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls are evident, given that women and girls are disproportionately affected by domestic and sexual abuse.  All efforts made to address domestic and sexual abuse for everyone under the new strategy will directly contribute to the broader goals of ending violence against women and girls. However, I recognise that violence perpetrated against women and girls is not restricted to situations of domestic and sexual abuse and can involve wider issues. We also collectively need to tackle the attitudes on which this violence is predicated. There is a role for the Department of Justice in this, and my Department is working with PSNI and the Executive Office on plans for a campaign to do this. However, the wider, societal and attitudinal issues reinforce the need for the Executive’s Strategic Framework to End Violence Against Women and Girls, in parallel with the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy.My officials worked closely with the Executive Office on the development of the EVAWG Framework and are committed to continued collaboration and partnership in order that both Strategies will complement each other. The governance structures will ensure alignment and support ongoing joint efforts to protect everyone.My Department, working with PSNI and the Department of Health has identified a number of measures that we believe would help to enhance protections for women and girls who are at risk of harm from domestic abuse. These include:the introduction of Domestic Abuse Protection Notices and Orders; investment in services to address offending behaviour;measures to enhance multi-agency work to assess and mitigate the risk posed to victims of domestic abuse; investment in technologies that would support the PSNI response to domestic abuse; and measures to reinforce the ability of Health and Social Care to support those at risk.However, for each of these additional resource would need to be made available to enable delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of victims of child sexual abuse in foster care, who are now adult survivors, have reported this to his Department since 2007.
answer_text: Via the Serious Adverse Incident Reporting Procedure, the Department of Health has been notified by HSC Trusts of 12 incidents of children with a primary residence of foster care being the victim of child sexual abuse, who are now adult survivors.  The SAI Reporting Procedure transferred to HSCB, current SPPG in 2010 and this response reflects the number of SAI notifications, whereby the Trust have coded the incident as sexual assault using the Datix Common Classification System (CCS) codes relevant to this question from May 2010 to October 2024. Please note, this figure does not indicate the location of where the abuse occurred or who perpetrated the abuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will bring forward his Department’s social inclusion strategies.
answer_text: I have decided to progress the Social Inclusion Strategies on a phased basis, with work commencing on the Anti-Poverty and Disability Strategies immediately. Details of the proposed timetable for the next steps in developing the Gender Equality and Sexual Orientation Strategies will follow in due course.Whilst my Department is leading on the Executive’s Social Inclusion Strategies, final decisions on their content, publication and implementation will be subject to Executive agreement and public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will bring forward the Anti-Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my previous answer to this topic in AQW 16628/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans for in-patient mental health and addiction services to be provided in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust area.
answer_text: Belfast Trust provides adult mental health inpatient facilities across 2 sites both in Belfast. These are within the Knockbracken Healthcare Park and Belfast City Hospital sites.The Belfast HSC Trust have the following commissioned inpatient facilities for the assessment and treatment of Mental illness;80 Acute Mental Health Inpatient beds based at Belfast City Hospital site. 34 Medium Secure Inpatient Forensic Mental Health beds, Shannon Clinic, based at Knockbracken Healthcare Park. This is a Regional Unit managed by BHSCT on behalf of other HSCTs, and;14 Mental Health Rehabilitation inpatient beds, on Clare Ward, based at Knockbracken Healthcare Park.All inpatient facilities offer specialised assessment and treatment to individuals presenting with mental ill health where their needs cannot be met in a community setting. In relation to drug and alcohol in-patient services, the Belfast Trust do not have any commissioned inpatient services for the sole treatment of addiction or detox intervention. Where a patient’s needs cannot be met by community services, there is a regional referral pathway which enables patients from the Belfast Trust to access inpatient beds based within Tier 4 Regional inpatient services for drug and alcohol. These are located in NHSCT (Holywell Hospital), SEHSCT (Downshire Hospital) and WHSCT (Tyrone and Fermanagh Hospital).The Belfast Trust also have commissioned residential rehabilitation beds within Carlisle House in Belfast and can signpost service users to other residential rehabilitation beds across the Region.The Belfast Trust are currently awaiting the recommendations from the Independent Review of Tier 4 Services which will indicate future planning in the area of in-patient provision and residential rehabilitation in relation to substance use services. Patients who have drug and alcohol issues and a co-existing mental illness can receive treatment for both whilst inpatient in any of the above-named facilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of housing units that (a) his Department; (b) the Housing Executive; and (c) Housing Associations will build in Belfast as part of the Belfast City Council Strategic Site Assessment process; and (ii) the locations of these housing units.
answer_text: Under the auspices of the Belfast Agenda, the Community Plan for the Belfast council district, Belfast City Council has established a working group to support delivery of the Plan’s ‘Housing Led Regeneration’ action plan through Strategic Site Assessment. My officials, together with the NIHE are collaborating with the Council on this city-wide assessment. At this juncture it is too soon to commit to the number of housing units that may be delivered or where they might be located.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the development of a site in North Belfast for a post-primary Irish-medium school.
answer_text: My Department commissioned Land and Property Services and the Strategic Investment Board to undertake site searches for a post-primary Irish-medium school in the greater North Belfast area. However, no suitable sites have been identified to date.To address the current accommodation pressures at Coláiste Feirste, the Department is working with the school and key stakeholders on plans to develop accommodation on an adjacent site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the reason for the latest delay in opening the new maternity hospital at Royal Victoria Hospital.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on how his Department can support the continuation of the Community Navigators Project in the RVH Emergency Department.
To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Public Land for Public Housing in the provision of social housing.
answer_text: The Public Land for Housing Pilot Scheme finished in March 2021.  The use of public land to support future housing supply is a central part of the draft Housing Supply Strategy which I am currently reviewing. Earlier this year, the Department approved a Northern Ireland Housing Executive Land Acquisition (HELA) business case which aims to identify and acquire land in public ownership to support the continued development of social housing through the Social Housing Development Programme.  The initial pilot is to identify sufficient land to deliver 100 units of social housing by 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of people deemed to be in housing stress, broken down by constituency; and (ii) the number of new housing builds in 2024-25, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 14815/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps he is taking to get additional medical and clinical evidence to support the provision of puberty blockers for young people aged under-18 attending the Gender Identity Services Clinic.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of children and young people awaiting percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy insertion surgery in the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children; and (ii) the waiting time for this surgery; and (iii) how families and the children and young people awaiting the surgery are being supported.
answer_text: The issue of access to percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) insertion for children and young people is an important issue as is the range of all surgical procedures that face children and young people in Northern Ireland.  This procedure is a complex procedure that requires specialised clinical and nursing input prior to, during and post procedure.  There are 93 children currently on the waiting list. Each case is constantly assessed by the specialist team at the RBHSC and dealt with as quickly as possible based on clinical priority and chronological order.  Due to the introduction of a new Encompass ICT system at BHSCT it is not possible to examine the data further at this stage.  It is hoped that when the Encompass system is fully operational across all Trusts in NI that the extraction and publication of data will be more streamlined.For example, at the moment a paediatric surgeon is validating the list so waiting times on the list may change dependent on the clinical priority. This validation process will identity which children are suitable for possible transfer to Alder Hey Hospital in Liverpool for the procedure.  Clinical criteria, agreed by the specialist team, will be in place to determine this.  BHSCT and Alder Hey are discussing the feasibility of this initiative.Belfast Trust are also hoping to provide a waiting list initiative in October, to carry out additional sessions in October, with patients being nursed in RBHSC over the weekend.  As well as this, discussions are underway to provide the Belfast Trust paediatric surgeon a gastrostomy surgery list in the Ulster Hospital.  Belfast Trust Gastrostomy Specialist Nurses will provide training to staff in South Eastern Trust to help facilitate this if it can arranged.There is also a review of surgical work performed in RBHSC underway and any work that can be redirected to District General Hospitals will be transferred.  This may free up capacity in RBHSC for Paediatric Surgeons to perform more gastrostomy surgery and other more complex surgery that can only be carried out in RBHSC.The Trust are also examining potential partnership working with the Private Sector.  This would mean using RBHSC capacity over the weekend to deliver Waiting List Initiatives in the Children’s Hospital and will be dependent on unscheduled care pressures. My Officials will continue to meet with BHSCT to discuss these potential solutions to examine their progress and success.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline his Department's plans to reduce housing stress in areas where there is high demand.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the process to appoint the Commissioners under the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: TEO is taking forward the necessary arrangements to implement the provisions of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 for which it is responsible, including the establishment of the new bodies, namely: The Office of Identity and Cultural Expression;The Irish Language Commissioner; andThe Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition.We have made our initial decisions regarding the recruitment process, further advice is being developed to enable the competitions to be launched. We will keep Members updated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any research his Department is undertaking to better understand the rising numbers of children with special educational needs.
answer_text: Overall policy responsibility for Special Educational Needs (SEN) rests with the Department for Education (DE), including decisions on research to explore population changes. The increasing population and complexity of need of children with SEN does have a direct impact on Health and Social Care. My officials are working with DE to undertake exploratory work on the use of Early Years data to better understand the increasing population and to develop robust projections for the coming academic years. This work is critical for long term service planning and workforce development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) how Deanby Primary School, North Belfast, will meet the physical and emotional needs of pupils with special educational needs and disability in September 2024; (ii) whether the school will have the infrastructure in place to ensure that (a) occupational therapy; (b) security; (c) dining; and (d) transport needs are met in September 2024; and (iii) whether the play areas will be designed to meet the children’s physical and emotional needs in September 2024.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is undertaking a programme of works in July and August at the site of the former Our Lady’s Primary School (PS).  Works include refurbishment of eight classrooms and corridors, the creation of sensory, therapy and hygiene rooms and external safeguarding works including fencing and canopies.The site has a fully operational production kitchen and dining room and a full complement of catering staff to allow provision of school meals from the start of the new term.The EA will provide transport assistance for pupils who have had a special transport need identified during the statementing process and a transport referral has been made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for a breakdown of the funding awarded for special educational needs and disabilities in youth services in North Belfast in the last five years, broken down by location.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that it supports Children and Young People with Special Educational Needs and Disabilities (SEND) through inclusive Youth Work Provision across Statutory, Community and Voluntary Sectors.The EA has further advised that it does not collate information to determine resources allocated specifically to support children and young people with SEND who attend these youth services.In addition, to meet the assessed needs of children and young people, a Belfast city-wide local area-based youth work funding award, held by Kids Together, enables the provision of youth work support to children and young people with SEND.  This funding stream was released in 2023 – ‘Developing Centre Based Youth Work’ with actual and planned funding as follows:Year 1 (2023-24) - £50,884Year 2 (2024-25) - £52,176Year 3 (2025-26) - £53,507

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the support that is in place for pupils with a classroom assistant in post-16 provision.

To ask the Minister of Education for an update on a new build school for Our Lady of Lourdes Primary School, Park Lodge, Belfast.
answer_text: Our Lady of Lourdes Primary School is proceeding under the School Enhancement Programme (SEP).The project is at Technical Design (RIBA Stage 4), and involves the construction of a new extension, the removal of temporary accommodation, make good hard play areas and provision of parking for visitors and staff. Funding is available to take the project through the planning and design stages to pre-tender stage. A decision to release to tender will be taken based on the budget position at that time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what his Department is doing to support children, young people and their families, who have Irish as their first language, going through the process of getting a diagnosis for autism.
answer_text: I thank the Member for her question, my Department work closely with HSC Trusts to provide health services that best meet the needs of service users. This includes providing services in the first language of the service user. My Department offers a range of interpreting services, through the Business Service Organisation (BSO), including through the medium of Irish, from a panel of self-employed interpreters. When contacted by an HSC Trust, BSO will outline the availability of Interpreter / language. If an interpreter is not available on the date requested, BSO will suggest potential alternative dates/times where an Interpreter may be available. If an interpreter for the requested language is not available BSO will provide guidance on alternative ways to access language support including: accessing a BSO Interpreter via video call (if BSO have the language); telephone Interpreting via Big Word; Face to Face Interpreting via Flex Language ServicesIn the absence of an interpreter the child or young person can bring a trusted adult with them to interpret for them to support the assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on a new build school for St Vincent de Paul Primary School, Belfast.
answer_text: The new build project for St Vincent de Paul Primary School is currently at the detailed design stage (RIBA Stage 3).  A planning application has been submitted for a new ten class base school and nursery unit on a site at Mill Avenue, Belfast. Funding is available to take the project through the design stages to pre-tender stage. A decision to release to tender will be taken based on the budget position at that time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department’s plans to address the need for a second post-primary Irish-medium school in the Belfast area.
answer_text: The establishment of a new school would require the publication of a Development Proposal as detailed in ‘Guidance on the Publication of a Development Proposal’ The Department funds Comhaire na Gáelscolaiochta to work with parent groups and Boards of Governors in the Irish-Medium sector to provide support in matters relating to Area Planning.  This includes to;provide information, analysis and advice on proposals impacting on their schools in Strategic Area Plans and Operational Plans; engage with the Education Authority and Council for Catholic Maintained Schools in identifying innovative, creative and shared solutions for sustainable provision;  engage fully with all other sectors in the planning process, with a view to adding to quality and viability of provision; andto provide assistance in the bringing forward of Development Proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans there are for extending post-16 special educational needs provision in North Belfast.
answer_text: Article 6 of the 1986 Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order places a duty on the Education Authority (EA) to secure provision of education, with particular regard to securing Special Educational Provision (SEP) for pupils who have Special Educational Needs (SEN).The EA’s Special Education Strategic Area Plan (SESAP) 2022-27 sets a five-year strategic direction.  The objectives of Special Education Area Planning are set out in Special Education Area Planning Frameworks and seek to enable provision for children and young people from early years through to post-primary including post-16.  The SESAP is underpinned by Operational Plans.  The first Operational Plan (OP1) covered the period September 2022–August 2024 and the next Operational Plan is currently being drafted.  Operational Plans cover all localities including Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans there are for the Harberton North special educational needs provision to be made permanent in North Belfast.
answer_text: On 13 October 2023 the Permanent Secretary approved DP 702 – Harberton School will change its designation from a 3-11 special school to operate as a 3-19 special school, across a dual campus, with effect from 1 September 2023 or as soon as possible thereafter. The 3-19 school will provide for up to 500 special school places on a phased basis over both campuses – South Harberton Park and North (Fortwilliam Park). The Education Authority as proposer has the responsibility of implementing the proposal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his Department's plans to extend the provision of special educational needs places in North Belfast.
answer_text: The legislative duty to place children with special educational needs (SEN) rests with the Education Authority (EA) as outlined in the Education (Northern Ireland) orders 1986 and 1996. Additional school places for children with SEN are being established across North Belfast for September 2024, in both special schools and through the establishment of specialist provision in mainstream classes.Given the immediate pressures on SEN placements for September 2024, my department has supported the EA by issuing a survey to all schools in April 2024 seeking their interest in establishing specialist provision, followed by joint engagement visits to schools who indicated they could establish new provision for September 2024, including in North Belfast.  Officials have written to all remaining schools to continue this engagement in the Autumn term 2024/25 in order to support the EA to plan for provision for September 2025 and beyond.Area planning for education, including for special education, is also the responsibility of the EA.  The EA’s Special Education Strategic Area Plan (SESAP) 2022-27 sets a five-year strategic direction.  This is underpinned by operational plans to support implementation.   Operational Plan 2 (2024-26) (OP2) is currently being developed and will prioritise planning for special education provision.  My department has requested that the EA includes in OP2 detailed plans for special education provision in line with projected need, for both special schools and specialist provisions in mainstream.I announced a SEN capital programme in April 2024.  This will be informed by the EA’s area planning along with the actions arising from the joint engagement visits planned for term one of the new academic year.  In particular, my department has asked the EA to provide a masterplan for each special school to ensure planning is in line with projected need for special education provision.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether it intends to provide enhanced facilities in Parliament Buildings to promote a healthy lifestyle.
To ask the Minister for Communities to provide details of the revitalisation of the Housing Executive.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Housing Executive is faced with a significant investment challenge. Transformation of the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, particularly focused on securing its access to borrowing, provides the opportunity to sustain public housing without acting as a drag on already stretched public services. I am carefully considering advice and options to transform it onto a sustainable footing to increase routine & planned investment, to deal with maintenance backlogs and a program of retrofit to improve energy efficiency in homes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of Serious Adverse Incidents declared in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust due to gaps in staff cover in child and family services, since February 2024.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) whether he has received the strategic priorities from Foras na Gaeilge; and (ii) whether he will ensure these strategic priorities are published.
answer_text: I have received Foras na Gaeilge’s Corporate Plan for 2022-2025 which, in line with the requirements of the establishing legislation and the Financial Memorandum for North/South Implementation Bodies, sets out Foras’ strategic objectives and the activities to be carried out to support the achievement of those objectives.Foras na Gaeilge have temporarily removed their 2022-2025 Corporate Plan from their website as it is being updated following NSMC approval on 29 May 2024. The CEO has confirmed that the approved version of the plan will be published on their website by  21 June 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether new homes will be built on an objective needs basis, with particular regard for areas of highest need.
answer_text: The Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) is a three-year rolling programme of planned social housing schemes based on the identification and analysis of housing need by geographical area. The formulation of the SHDP is shaped by the Housing Executive’s Strategic Guidelines which ensure that the Programme is distributed equitably across a range of programme policy areas and targets those areas of greatest social housing need.In managing the SHDP, the Housing Executive administers Housing Association Grant on behalf of DfC to Housing Associations to facilitate the delivery of social housing and directs them to identify and bring forward development opportunities in those areas of greatest unmet social housing need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail the number of children living in emergency accommodation; and (ii) for a breakdown of the geographical areas in which this accommodation is situated.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin October 2023 – March 2024, published on 13 June, contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-october-2023-march-2024 Table 3.3 details children in temporary accommodation by type of accommodation.  	The Housing Executive has previously advised my officials that it does not maintain a list of non-standard placements (Hotel/B&B) by the area to which households are placed. Therefore, I am unable to provide this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how the Public Health Agency will ensure that procurement of contracts for services on mental health will be accessible to, and inclusive of, providers based within the community and voluntary sector.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency advertise all funding opportunities on its website and through eTendersNI which is an online portal used by all Government Departments, agencies and arms-length bodies. PHA would advise organisations wanting to avail of future funding opportunities to register on this platform.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when his Department will support stem cell donor awareness.
answer_text: Raising awareness about stem cell donation is generally led by a number of charities through UK-wide communications and campaigns which provide information about the signs and symptoms of blood cancer and other blood disorders, and inform people about how they can join the network of national and international stem cell registries.  Registering as a stem cell donor can potentially help a patient in another part of the world for whom a related donor match has not been identified.  My Department has supported previous campaigns to raise awareness about stem cell donation, and plans to do so again this September for Blood Cancer Awareness Month.  It is hoped that this will encourage informed discussion and positive action around this important subject.The Department’s Arm’s Length Bodies, including the Public Health Agency, Belfast Health and Social Care Trust and the Northern Ireland Blood Transfusion Service have also supported stem cell donation awareness through signposting and providing information about joining the stem cell registries, and promoting the campaign activities of charities and individual families.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on when dual diagnosis treatment and care will be established in Emergency Departments.
answer_text: Addressing the harm related to alcohol and other drugs is a key priority for my Department. My Department and officials lead on the Northern Ireland Executive's 10-year Substance Use Strategy Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery. This strategy aims to directly prevent and reduce the harm related to substance use through a range of cross-departmental action and support.To address a key action within the strategy, the Public Health Agency and my Department have co-produced, across all tiers of service, a new outcomes-focused Substance Use Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan.  The issue of co-occurring mental health and addiction problems, is recognised not only by the Substance Use Strategy and Strategic Plan but is also a key action within the Mental Health Strategy.Both Strategies recommend the creation of a “Managed Care Network with experts in dual diagnosis, supporting and building capacity in both mental health and substance use services, to ensure that these services meet the full needs of those with co-occurring issues”. In line with this recommendation, recurrent funding of circa £70k per annum has been provided to appoint a Co-occurring Mental Health & Substance Use Project Manager. Recruitment for this post is underway and it is hoped that this investment will lead to enhanced and integrated pathways between Mental Health and Substance Use services to ensure timely access to the treatment and support that a person needs to maximise their recovery.The Department is working to reinforce linkages with the Mental Health Strategy’s Early Intervention & Action Plan, the development of the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service, and the development of the Regional Mental Health Service, to devise better pathways of care and models of support for those in Mental Health Crisis with co-occurring addiction issues.In addition to the above, the Strategic Performance and Planning Group (SPPG), and the Public Health Agency (PHA), have developed the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service Implementation Plan 2021-31, to support people in mental health crisis with co-occurring addiction issues through the following key objectives:A regionally consistent crisis service that will provide effective help and support for people in a crisisA reduction in the number of people who have to wait longer than 2 hours for crisis support as laid out in the Regional You in Mind Mental Health Care Pathway.A reduction in the number of people who attend Emergency Departments in crisis.In line with the usual process for commissioning of services, any decision to commission mental health crisis services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people currently awaiting a domiciliary care package in (i) North Belfast; and (ii) West Belfast.
answer_text: The number of people currently awaiting a domiciliary care package from the Belfast Trust in (i) North Belfast is 54 and in (ii) West Belfast is 77.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how his Department will address long autism diagnosis waiting times; and (ii) how his Department will assist in the autism diagnosis of children and young people in (a) North Belfast; and (b) West Belfast.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will extend the contract of the Truth Recovery Independent Panel.
answer_text: We have received the Truth Recovery Independent Panel’s Interim Report and are considering the five recommendations.  Regarding the recommendation to extend the Panel’s working remit by six months, we are considering, and will formally respond to the Independent Panel regarding this matter in due course.In considering, we will balance the need for the investigation to proceed in a timely way, with the need to make sure the largest number of people possible have an opportunity to provide their oral testimony to the Independent Panel.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why there are no services and support to assist the statementing process for autism through the medium of Irish language for children and young people who have Irish as their first language.
answer_text: Statutory assessment of all children and young people is conducted by the Educational Psychology Service in the Education Authority (EA).  For children in Irish-medium education (IME), where possible the EA attempts to provide educational psychologists with experience in Irish language or bilingualism.There are a range of Special Educational Needs (SEN) services and supports available to all schools, including Irish-medium schools.  Curriculum resources for schools for the relevant areas of learning to support pupils are available in Irish on the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) website.Middletown Centre for Autism also provides workforce training, one-to-one support for pupils, online resources and training sessions for parents all delivered through the medium of Irish.The department is engaging with Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CnaG) and the IME sector to ensure that the specific needs of the sector are considered as part of the End-to-End (E2E) Review of SEN.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how his Department will ensure that homes are built on the basis of objective need for areas that have the highest levels of housing stress.
answer_text: The Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) is a three-year rolling programme of planned social housing schemes based on the identification and analysis of housing need by geographical area. The formulation of the SHDP is shaped by the Housing Executive’s Strategic Guidelines which ensure that the Programme is distributed equitably across a range of programme policy areas and targets those areas of greatest social housing need.In managing the SHDP, the Housing Executive administers Housing Association Grant on behalf of DfC to Housing Associations to facilitate the delivery of social housing and directs them to identify and bring forward development opportunities in those areas of greatest unmet social housing need.My Department remains committed to the provision of new homes for those on the social housing waiting list despite other financial pressures. The Department continues to direct the majority of its capital budget spend on the delivery of new social homes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department's plans to recruit staff for social services to fill the 81 vacant posts in North Belfast and West Belfast.
answer_text: My Department has been working collaboratively with all of the Trusts and other stakeholders to address the high level of vacancies in social work services. In an effort to stabilise the HSC social work workforce, a policy decision was taken to cease the use of recruitment agency social workers by the end of June 2023. Former recruitment agency social workers were encouraged to apply for posts in the Trusts and many of them availed of that opportunity. Between June and September 2023, Trusts also undertook a large recruitment exercise to attract almost 200 newly qualified social workers into vacant Trust posts. A similar exercise is underway at present, to recruit a large number of newly qualified social workers into Trust vacancies in the summer of 2024. It is anticipated that Belfast Trust will appoint approximately 60 newly qualified social workers in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the support provided to the Victims and Survivors Consultation Forum; and (ii) why the contact details of all the victims and survivors of the Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses have not been provided to the Chairperson of the Victims and Survivors Consultation Forum.
answer_text: SupportThe Consultation Forum has been an important part of the Truth Recovery process to date and our focus has been on making them as safe as possible for everyone in attendance.The Forum has met on thirty formal occasions with regular engagement and correspondence in between meetings. Since its inception, additional support measures have evolved to make it a safer space for all, with a focus on meeting the needs of victims-survivors. The measures have included but are not limited to, support from secretariat officials who provide support before, during, and after these meetings; appointment of a voluntary Independent Chairperson in early 2022; and a part time clinical psychologist since August 2023.  The Forum has an agreed Terms of Reference, including a Meeting Protocol (which covers confidentiality, conduct and standards), structured agendas, comprehensive minutes (which includes contributions made via the ‘Chat’ function).  Victims-survivors can avail of professional emotional support from a clinical psychologist, Victims and Survivors Service, and WAVE Trauma counsellors during meetings; and this support is also available via a dedicated phone line for several hours after each meeting, if in the event, any Member is of need of emotional support.  There is also an opportunity for a group debrief with the clinical psychologist and victims-survivors at the end of every meeting.Following a structured review of the Forum, throughout May and June 2024, the Independent Chair and clinical psychologist conducted a series of workshops with victims-survivors on the purpose, priorities and structures of the Forum, which includes thoughts on advocacy. We are conscious the support plans may need to change as the process evolves and are committed to keeping this under review based on needs.Mailing list The Independent Chair has accepted this role on a voluntary basis and their primary function is to chair the monthly Forum meeting which is an online meeting for 2 hours each month. For capacity, information security and confidentiality reasons – a small number of TEO officials manage a mailbox and mailing list for the Forum of approximately 80 individuals. This list was received from the Design Panel and can change with people opting in and out based on their choice. All communications are issued on a blind carbon copy basis. If the Independent Chair makes a specific request for an individual’s contact details (and vice versa), officials contact the individual in question seeking consent that they are content to share.  Officials also regularly issue messages from the Forum Chair on their behalf. Truth Recovery Programme is open to different approaches in relation to this based on individual’s preferences and the need to comply with information security and statutory obligations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline his plans to address addiction and mental health treatment and care in Belfast.
answer_text: Addressing the harm related to alcohol and other drugs is a key priority for my Department. My Department and officials lead on the Northern Ireland Executive's 10-year Substance Use Strategy Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery. This strategy aims to directly prevent and reduce the harm related to substance use through a range of cross-departmental action and support.To address a key action within the strategy, the Public Health Agency and my Department have co-produced, across all tiers of service, a new outcomes-focused Substance Use Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan.  Following extensive consultation, the Substance Use Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan is due for publication this month and implementation of the identified priorities will be progressed in line with the work of the Regional Mental Health Service and the Integrated Care System.The issue of co-occurring mental health and addiction problems, is recognised not only by the Substance Use Strategy and Strategic Plan but is also a key action within the Mental Health Strategy. Therefore, my department is working at my request to reinforce linkages with the Mental Health Strategy’s Early Intervention & Action Plan, the development of the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service, and the development of the Regional Mental Health Service, to devise better pathways of care and models of support for those in Mental Health Crisis and with co-occurring addiction issues.Both Strategies recommend the creation of a “Managed Care Network’ with experts in mental health and substance use, to support and build capacity in and across services, to ensure that the needs of those with co-occurring issues are fully met. In line with this recommendation, recurrent funding has been provided to appoint a Co-occurring Mental Health & Substance Use Project Manager. Recruitment for this post is underway and it is hoped that this investment will lead to enhanced and integrated pathways between Mental Health and Substance Use services to ensure timely access to the treatment and support that a person needs to maximise their recovery.In addition, I note that Belfast Trust is currently involved in a number of innovation and modernisation projects that will deliver greater capacity for new patient appointments and speedier referrals. These projects are in the absence of additional funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on suicide prevention infrastructure on bridges along the Westlink and M2 areas of Belfast.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of developing measures to reduce the risk of suicide at high-risk locations on our infrastructure.	My Department has designed and tested a barrier system to prevent access at Clifton and Divis Street Bridges, as part of a pilot project.	My officials are currently preparing a business case for this project and finalising the contract documents to enable the fabrication and erection of the barrier.	Delivery of the project will depend on funding being provided being to my Department.	My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment and the funding pressures that we are currently facing means that it is unlikely that this project will be delivered in this financial year.  However, I am continuing to bid for the necessary funding to deliver this important project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what actions his Department and the Education Authority are taking to ensure that children with special educational needs get a school placement in September.
answer_text: Ensuring that all children with a statement of special educational needs (SEN) have a school placement that is appropriate to meet their needs, is and will continue to be a primary focus for my department and the Education Authority (EA).My department and the EA collectively are taking a range of actions to ensure sufficient and appropriate capacity is created and maintained across the entire education system to support learners who require special education provision.These actions include the recent survey issued by my department to assess mainstream schools’ capacity and ability to establish specialist provisions, which received over 700 responses, with almost 100 schools willing to establish new provision in time for September 2024 and a further 300 schools for September 2025. The department and the EA working collaboratively have undertaken an intense programme of engagement with those schools, which has initially focussed on areas of known high demand and where provision can be established in time for September 2024.  Following this, further engagement will take place with schools to develop plans for September 2025 and beyond. A SEN placements ‘taskforce’, chaired by the Chief Executive of the EA, has been formed to bring together the SEN placements, policy, estates and capital teams from across both organisations and meets weekly to ensure close monitoring of the position and collaborative action.Good progress has been made:  as of 24 June, 83% of children with a statement of SEN have now had confirmation of their school placement for September 2024.  95.5% of primary 7 children have a confirmed place and the EA is working at pace to secure places for children in other year groups.  Bearing in mind that each child is placed individually, this figure increases on a daily basis as children’s places are confirmed.  Despite this, the situation remains challenging and the EA supported by my department is continuing to push forward with a focused effort to increase capacity within both special and mainstream schools to meet the demand for September 2024 and beyond.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline how social value policies are being developed within her Department.
To ask the Minister for the Economy whether plans for economic regional balance includes addressing economic opportunities in communities that experience multiple deprivation.
answer_text: Regional Balance is one of my key priorities, with the aim to provide access to economic opportunities to all in the North, including communities that experience multiple deprivation. 		I have set out my Sub Regional Economic Plan for achieving this goal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has raised the issue of the PSNI surveillance of journalists and solicitors with the Chief Constable.
answer_text: I have spoken with the Chief Constable on this matter.While this is an operational policing matter, and not all within the devolved sphere, I share the concerns raised around the routine surveillance of journalists and solicitors.  However, the PSNI is accountable to the Policing Board and it is important to allow the Policing Board the opportunity to consider this issue fully in the first instance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the steps he is taking to protect Neighbourhood Renewal projects.
answer_text: I recognise the important role that the Neighbourhood Renewal projects play in delivering critical services and supporting those communities, groups and individuals who are amongst the most vulnerable in society.  I also understand the impact that increasing costs, budget uncertainty and financial constraints have on the groups, their staff and on those who need their support.   As an interim measure, and in advance of the Executive agreeing a budget, I provided budget cover for the period up to 30 June for Neighbourhood Renewal groups.  This provided the groups with an interim budget based on 25% of their 2023/24 opening allocation.The grant funding payments were provided to the Neighbourhood Renewal groups in early April. I also provided an additional funding allocation of £1500 (per group) in March 2024 to every Neighbourhood Renewal group to help them meet increasing costs, including running and programme costs. You will be aware that the Executive has now agreed a budget and I am working at pace with my Senior Team within the Department to consider the overall budget position to inform the decisions that need to be made to ensure the Department can live within its allocation while delivering my priorities. In the longer term, my officials will continue to support the work of the Joint Forum to develop a set of principles aimed at improving the funding relationship across the Voluntary and Community Sector, including organisations funded through Neighbourhood Renewal.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their plans to implement the recommendations of the Commission on Flags, Identity, Culture and Tradition Final Report.
answer_text: On 22 April 2024 Officials met with Junior Ministers to discuss matters in relation to the report. Officials are preparing a submission which is expected to set out a number of options for consideration regarding the next steps in relation to the report and its recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline how his Department will promote Community Wealth Building.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he is considering a residents' parking scheme for residents living beside the Ulster University campus in North Belfast.
answer_text: I am aware of concerns about parking in residential areas in the vicinity of colleges and universities.The review of residents’ parking schemes is complete, and the report will be published when I have considered the findings, which I expect to be in the coming months. Until such time, I am not in a position to comment on specific requests for further schemes.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for a timescale on when religious orders and Barnardo's will contribute to the redress scheme for victims and survivors of institutional abuse.
answer_text: Two institutions, Barnardo’s and the Good Shepherd Sisters, intend to make interim contributions in the coming weeks.  Engagement will continue on final contributions following the closure of the HIA Redress Scheme.  The value of the final contributions will not be known until all applications made to the HIA Redress Board, by the closing date of 2 April 2025, have been determined.The Independent Facilitator, Mr Paul Sweeney, drafted a framework for reaching an agreement between the Executive and the six voluntary institutions, which we approved in 2021.  The Historical Institutional Abuse Inquiry Report recommended that any voluntary institution found by the Inquiry to have been guilty of systemic failings should be asked to make an appropriate financial contribution to the overall cost of the HIA Redress Board and any specialist support services. Constructive discussions on appropriate contributions from the other four institutions are ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether they will consider placing distance markers on floors or walls of corridors to inform people who walk in Parliament Buildings as a form of exercise of the distance they have walked.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission recognises this is a positive suggestion seeking to promote a healthy and active environment, a commitment the Assembly Commission shares.  Given that Parliament Buildings is a Grade A listed building, the Assembly Commission has to be mindful of the architectural integrity and heritage of the building. Therefore, placing distance markers on floors or walls of corridors would be problematic.However, the Assembly Commission will give consideration to other ways of highlighting the distances around the building.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the waiting list for reconstructive surgery for women who have underwent surgery for breast cancer in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: This data is not held by the Department. Information has been provided by the Belfast HSC Trust.Breast reconstruction surgery for breast cancer can be offered as either an immediate procedure as part of the cancer diagnosis pathway or as a planned procedure some years after the patient’s original breast cancer surgery. Those patients undergoing immediate reconstruction are dealt with acutely and fall within the 62 day cancer treatment pathway. The number of patients that are currently on the waiting list for a delayed reconstruction are 23 with longest waiter from 2016. There are an additional 69 patients that are waiting for adjustments to a previous reconstruction with the longest wait since 2016. These patients are within the routine planned surgery category. The service is currently revalidating the long waiters that include this group of patients. The table below summaries the number waiting in weeks/years as requested. Table 1. Patients Waiting for Breast Reconstruction and Reconstruction Adjustments in the Belfast HSC Trust by Weeks Waiting, April 2024TypeWeeks WaitingTotal<13=13-21=21-26=26-39=39-52>52Reconstruction0<5<50<51923Adjustments<5<505<55469Total<55<5557392Source: Belfast HSC Trust	Numbers less than 5 are changed to ‘<5´ to mask individuals who may be identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide details of the (i) addiction programmes; and (ii) associated support that is available to all those in prison or a youth detention centre.
answer_text: Addictions services and programmes for people in prison in Northern Ireland are provided on behalf of the Department of Health by the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (the Trust). The Trust commissioned Start 360 to provide drug counselling and therapeutic interventions on its behalf.Within Prisons, individuals who want help with tackling the use of alcohol or drugs will be referred to the Start 360 Team. Within the Youth Justice Agency, all children who are placed in the Juvenile Justice Centre for longer than 24 hours have a medical assessment which includes substance use issues. A Youth Justice Agency Needs Assessment is also undertaken, part of which examines more fully substance use issues, and informs an intervention plan where applicable.Individual and bespoke intervention plans (including medication solutions) are delivered by the Healthcare team (nursing), the Residential Social Work team and the onsite ACORN team. ACORN is a mental health/therapeutic service consisting of psychology, psychiatry and other therapeutic providers.  Substance use issues are dealt with as part of the child's wider systemic care plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what discussions he has had with Executive colleagues to introduce legislation to bring the regulation of funfairs under the responsibility of local councils.
answer_text: I have not had any discussions with Executive colleagues about introducing legislation to bring the regulation of funfairs under the responsibility of local councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline what action her Department is taking to address delays in issuing legal aid payments.
To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider an uplift to the amount awarded by the Housing Executive for Disability Grants for Adaption to homes.
answer_text: My Department in partnership with Housing Executive (NIHE), Department of Health (DoH) and service user interest groups, is in the preliminary stages of conducting a comprehensive review into the operation of mandatory and discretionary grants administered by NIHE. This review will include re-evaluating the amount that NIHE can award to Disabled Facilities Grant (DFG) applicants.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update in relation to securing funds for victims of the Historical Institutional Abuse Redress Scheme from (i) the Sisters of Nazareth; (ii) De La Salle Order; (iii) Sisters of St Louis; (iv) Good Shepherd Sisters; (v) the Irish Church Missions; and (vi) Barnardo’s.
answer_text: The Good Shepherd Sisters made a contribution to the cost of the HIA Redress Scheme on 23 April, based on compensation paid to date. Barnardo’s will make a similar payment imminently. We are continuing to engage with Barnardo’s on receiving this payment in the coming weeks. Engagement will continue on final contributions following the closure of the HIA Redress Board. Constructive discussions on appropriate contributions from the other four institutions are ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how children and young people will be assessed for autism through the medium of Irish language.
answer_text: My Department does not systematically record this information. HSC Trusts are committed to providing services in the language that will best meet the needs of patients through interpretation services where this is required or asked for.The BSO Regional Interpreting Service does not have any registered Interpreters for face-to-face Irish language but Trusts can avail of other agency providers if Irish interpreting is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline his plans to reduce pollution in areas close to motorways and main roads in Belfast City Centre.
answer_text: Plans to reduce air pollution close to arterial roads in Belfast City Centre, and other congested locations, will form an important part of Northern Ireland’s first Clean Air Strategy.Officials have been progressing this work as far as possible in the absence of an Executive. Now that the Executive has returned, I will work with my officials and Ministerial colleagues to finalise this important cross-cutting strategy.It is worth noting, that many of the measures to reduce air pollution from transport and the road network, are the responsibility of the Department for Infrastructure, as a Competent Authority. DfI is currently implementing its Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan. The aim of this plan is to provide safe and attractive active travel infrastructure to enable more people in Belfast to choose walking, wheeling, and cycling for many of their everyday journeys.While I have yet to make my decision on the policies and actions to be included in the Clean Air Strategy, once finalised, this strategy will be central to improving air quality in Belfast City Centre, and Northern Ireland as a whole.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for a breakdown of funding awarded to (i) groups; and (ii) agencies who support victims of domestic violence, broken down by each constituency, in each of the last two years.
answer_text: Much of the work provided by organisations supporting victims of domestic violence and abuse is available to everyone across the whole of Northern Ireland and therefore cannot be broken down by geographical area.  For those cases where it is possible to provide a further breakdown, it should be noted that our current funding structure does not generally allow for a direct breakdown by constituency, but rather by council area.
