question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how many children and young people with disabilities have had a severe reaction to having teeth extracted in the Royal School of Dentistry since January 2020.
To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the Judicial Review judgement of 30 June 2023 on the 2010 criteria, what new criteria he will be bringing forward on Continuing Healthcare.
answer_text: The Judicial Review judgement of 30 June 2023, on the 2010 criteria, is currently under appeal by my Department. The issues raised are therefore subject to separate judicial consideration and it would not be appropriate for me to comment at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline how many people, via the Education Authority, are able to assess children and young people for ADHD and autism through the medium of Irish.
To ask the Minister of Finance to provide an update on gender budgeting within her Department.
To ask the Minister for Communities, following comments by the Speaker at the conclusion of Question Time on 12 March 2024, whether he will now answer questions relating to housing.
answer_text: In line with the Speaker’s comments, I will seek information from the Chief Executive of the Northern Ireland Housing Executive in relation to Assembly Questions about issues that are operational matters for the Housing Executive.However, Members may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly in relation to queries relating to operational matters. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that information is received in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.I will seek information from the Housing Association sector where necessary in relation to matters relating to my official responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when the social strategies that are within his responsibility will be published with a costed action plan.
answer_text: I want to ensure that the Executive’s Social Inclusion Strategies are sustainable, deliverable and make a meaningful difference to people’s lives particularly for those who are vulnerable and are affected by social isolation in all its forms.I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to the Strategies and final decisions on the content and timeline for implementation will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what legislation he plans to introduce to support residents who own defective homes deemed unsafe to live in but are still required to pay mortgages and utility bills.
answer_text: The Minister for Communities has confirmed that his department is the lead policy department with respect to building safety in Northern Ireland and has accepted the transfer of the 1975 Order to his department by way of a Transfer of Functions Order which my officials are currently progressing.	It will be for DfC officials to explore the way forward in relation to any changes in the limitation periods in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people are awaiting a domiciliary care package in North Belfast.
answer_text: The information requested is not held by constituency area. The latest available figures (as at 31 January 2024) for Belfast HSC Trust are shown in the table below.Clients awaiting a domiciliary care package in Belfast HSC Trust, as at 31 January 2024Type of packageNo. of clientsFull323Partial414Total737Source: Monthly returns supplied by HSC Trusts to PSSI, SPPGDomiciliary care services continue to experience significant pressures across the region, and there are a range of factors currently impacting upon delivery of this service; this includes significant retention issues in regard to domiciliary care staff.I recently announced a £70m funding package across a range of service provision, including domiciliary care. The purpose of this was to ensure independent sector providers had the necessary funding to meet the challenges of increases to national living wage from the beginning of April. I have also established structures to consider how we move the sector to a Real Living Wage employer base and significant work is ongoing to build the case for the additional funds that will be required to deliver this.Where delivery of care packages has been negatively impacted due to challenges regarding capacity, Trusts have in place mitigation measures to try to ensure services continue to be provided.My Department remains committed to the provision of a high quality domiciliary care service, and will continue to work alongside Trusts and independent sector providers to maintain and support service provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of Assured Skills academies.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline what dual diagnosis services are available in emergency departments for patients with poor mental health and addictions.

To ask the Minister for Communities whether he is going to include people who leave their home as a result of domestic violence within (i) the current approach; or (ii) any new approach to allocating points for social housing.
answer_text: Work has been ongoing in relation to the Fundamental Review of Social Housing Allocations. Under the current scheme, someone who leaves their home as a result of domestic abuse can be allocated points for homelessness, primary social needs or violence or the threat of violence. My officials are working with the Housing Executive to consider the way forward for the proposal relating to intimidation points. This includes independent research which is due to complete by the end of March 2024. Once options in relation to this proposal have been further developed, I will give them my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many (i) hostels; and (ii) emergency accommodation in Belfast are funded by his Department through the Housing Executive.
answer_text: The Belfast Trust has confirmed that they do not fund any (i) hostels; and (ii) emergency accommodation in Belfast through the Housing Executive.The Housing (NI) Order 1988 imposes a duty on NIHE relating to persons who are found to be homeless. The situation in relation to young homeless young people aged 16/17 is as follows. The Belfast HSC Trust has a duty to undertake an assessment and establish duties owed to a homeless young person aged 16 / 17 under Article 21 of the Children (NI) Order 1995.  The NIHE fund the emergency assessment beds for homeless young people for the 10-day assessment period. If a decision is made that a young person meets criteria under Article 21, the young person will then become looked after, and receive the same provisions as any Looked After Child and Belfast HSC Trust then funds the placement directly.  It should be noted that following assessment homeless children aged 16 and 17 with little exception, who cannot return home safely to family or relatives, would be deemed to need to become looked after.The Minister for Health has no responsibility to fund accommodation needs for those above 18 years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) when a draft Order will be laid in the Assembly to give effect to the 2022 final recommendations of the Local Government Boundary Commissioner; and (ii) whether this will be with or without modifications.
answer_text: The Final Recommendations Report of the Local Government Boundaries Commissioner, together with a draft Order to give effect to the recommendations and, if necessary, a statement of modifications will be laid in the Assembly as soon as possible following my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on (i) when the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be published; (ii) how much funding has been earmarked for the Anti-Poverty Strategy; and (iii) whether the Anti-Poverty Strategy will have objective need at the heart of any outcomes arising from this strategy.
answer_text: I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty in all its forms for our communities right across Northern Ireland.I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to the Anti-Poverty Strategy.  Final content of the Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Supporting People Programme.
answer_text: The Supporting People Programme is a vital service that supports vulnerable people, enabling them to have a better quality of life, to live more independently and to maintain their tenancies.  The programme continues to be delivered by the NIHE on behalf of my Department.  It currently provides housing related support services to over 19,000 vulnerable people in Northern Ireland. My Department and NIHE are engaging with the sector in these challenging times to seek ways to ensure the long term resilience of the Supporting People programme, secure value for money and to ensure the programme delivers real outcomes for service users – those most vulnerable in our society.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the (i) policy development; and (ii) investment of services for dual diagnosis for people in mental health crisis with addiction.
answer_text: Addressing the harm related to alcohol and other drugs is a key priority for my Department, which leads on the Northern Ireland Executive's 10-year Substance Use Strategy Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery. This strategy aims to directly prevent and reduce the harm related to substance use through a range of cross-departmental action and support. To address a key action within the strategy, the Public Health Agency and DoH Strategic Planning & Performance Group (SPPG) have co-produced, across all tiers of service, a new outcomes-focused strategic service delivery plan, to replace the current Alcohol & Drug Services Commissioning Framework. Following the recent public consultation, the Substance Use Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan is currently being reviewed for alignment with the work of the Regional Mental Health Service and the Integrated Care System. The issue of dual diagnosis, or co-occurring mental health and addiction problems, is also recognised in the Mental Health Strategy. Therefore, it is critical that we reinforce linkages with the Mental Health Strategy’s Early Intervention & Action Plan, the development of the Regional Mental Health Crisis Service, and the development of the Regional Mental Health Service, to devise better pathways and models for those in Mental Health Crisis and with co-occurring addiction issues.Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery and the Mental Health Strategy both recommend the creation of a “Managed Care Network with experts in dual diagnosis, supporting and building capacity in both mental health and substance use services, to ensure that these services meet the full needs of those with co-occurring issues”. In line with this recommendation, recurrent funding of circa £70k per annum has been provided to appoint a Co-occurring Mental Health & Substance Use Project Manager. Recruitment for this post is underway and it is hoped that this investment will lead to enhanced and integrated pathways between Mental Health and Substance Use services to ensure timely access to the treatment and support that a person needs to maximise their recovery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Boiler Replacement Scheme will be reopened for applications.
answer_text: My department has no plans to re-open the Boiler Replacement SchemeThe current Affordable Warmth Scheme has approval to run until March 2026 and includes providing grants for central heating systems.  The Scheme aims to assist low-income owner-occupiers and households in the private rented sector, with an annual household income of less than £23,000. Free comprehensive advice and support on how to save energy in the home and eligibility for help to improve energy efficiency is available from the NI Energy Advice Energy Advice Service via: 0800 1114455 or email: NIenergyadvice@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) a breakdown of funding for homelessness and addiction services in Belfast; (ii) where each service is based; and (iii) when confirmation of future funding will be awarded for homelessness services.
answer_text: Funding in relation to Homeless addiction services and where they are based is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  You should therefore direct your question to the Housing Executive’s dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk In relation to point (iii) of your question, the Executive will agree and set the 2024-25 budget.  Once the outcome is known, consideration will be given on how to allocate the Department’s budget against key priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how vulnerable tenants are supported in sustaining their tenancies with the Housing Executive and housing associations.
answer_text: How vulnerable tenants of the Housing Executive are supported in sustaining their tenancies is an operational matter for the Housing Executive as a social landlord.You should, therefore, direct your question with respect to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.The Department does not hold any information on sustaining tenancies with regards to Housing Associations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on funding for community pharmacies; and (ii) whether this funding model will reflect the rising cost of drugs.
answer_text: I very much appreciate the extremely important work that community pharmacies deliver for our population. I have received representation from pharmacy contractors and from their representative body, CPNI and an urgent meeting was held on 21 February 2024 with representatives of CPNI to consider the funding issues.I can assure the member that we are looking into a range of ways to stabilise the sector and that the funding model does include measures to address the rising cost of medicines. I have asked my Department to address some of the pressures that pharmacies are facing within what is available and affordable. I encouraged CPNI to continue to work with Department officials to seek ways to ensure the pharmacy service can develop in line with our shared strategic objectives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether groups in receipt of funding from his Department will receive an increase in salaries using National Joint Council payscales.
answer_text: My Department is not the direct employer for staff in any of the groups which it funds, and therefore, from a contractual perspective, has no role in setting salaries or employment terms and conditions for staff employed by those groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the capital budget for his Department contains additional funds for refurbishments for Youth Centres that are not mainstream Education Authority.
answer_text: The Youth Capital Funding Scheme provides the vehicle for capital funding to voluntary youth organisations.  The scheme can be used to refurbish, extend or rebuild existing premises, or to assist with costs to comply with statutory requirements, such as Health and Safety, Child Protection, Fire, and Disability Discrimination Act standards.  The last call for applications for funding under this Scheme was in 2016.  Regrettably, a number of the approved projects within that call have not been able to move to construction and are paused at pre-tender stage as a result of the constrained Capital Budget position in recent years.    Substantial capital investment is urgently required right across the entire Education Estate, to allow me to release a broad range of projects that are currently paused, including those within the voluntary youth sector.  In my statement to the Assembly on 12 February, I stated that I will be making the case for additional and sustained capital investment in our education infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on when Multi-Disciplinary teams will have a full staff compliment in North Belfast.
answer_text: The MDT model remains the key to stabilising and strengthening vital Primary Care services to ensure they can continue to provide high quality care to our people, now and into the future.  In March 2022, I announced that North Belfast would be one of the next three proposed GP Federation areas for roll-out of the MDT Programme, along with the South West (Fermanagh and Omagh) and East Antrim.Whilst the budget for 2024/25 has not yet been set it is expected that the overall financial position for all NI departments will remain very challenging. My Department remains committed to the further implementation of the MDT model across Northen Ireland, and is developing a plan for rollout over the next number of years subject to the availability of the necessary recurrent funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the plans he has to ensure children and young people receive assessments for Autism and ADHD; (ii) the proposed timeframe for these assessments; and (iii) the current waiting times for these assessments in (a) North Belfast; (b) South Belfast; (c) East Belfast; and (d) West Belfast.
answer_text: My Department is committed to improving delivery of services to young people with autism and ADHD and in the past 6 months officials have met with all Trusts to map current paediatric ASD and ADHD referral/assessment pathways. Following this mapping exercise, officials within my Department are working with colleagues from Public Health Agency to develop a regionally consistent pathway that complies with NICE guidelines. Work to develop this pathway will take into consideration best practice, emerging evidence and the recognition that workforce planning is critical to meeting need. My Department is committed to deliver ASD and ADHD pathways in line with NICE guidelines, this means assessments beginning within 13 weeks and being completed in a timely manner. You have asked about waiting times in specific areas of Belfast, unfortunately neither my Department nor the Belfast Trust compile information in these specific geographical areas. I can advise that at 31st December 2023 in BHSCT the waiting times and longest waits for Paediatric ASD and ADHD were as follows;Service Waiting Longest Wait Paed ASD 37144 years 6 monthsPaed ADHD 10294 years 9 monthsMy Department has recently published the cross-departmental Autism Strategy 2023-2028 including an action plan for implementation.   The Autism Strategy 2023 – 2028 Delivery Plan 2023-2025 includes an action to consult on and finalise a Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework which will guide future development of services. The Framework will embrace a move towards understanding Autism within a broader neurodevelopmental approach and incorporate a regionally consistent single point of entry and diagnostic pathway.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on health and social care for people who are street homeless in Belfast city.
answer_text: The Belfast Inclusion Service, within the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, is a nurse led service operating since 1999.  It provides access to primary health and social care services for those experiencing, or at risk of experiencing, homelessness in Belfast. The service includes a multidisciplinary team covering access to nurses, social workers, podiatry services, dental services, mental health support, support workers and a GP service.  The service also provides outreach to various locations, including the streets of Belfast.  This helps address some of the barriers to accessing primary care, experienced by people who are homeless. A trauma informed approach is used at the Belfast Inclusion Service to help empower service users to make informed choices about health and lifestyle decisions. In addition, the Public Health Agency (PHA) funds a range of resources to support those experiencing homelessness, including the Home Starter Pack Scheme, Resources for Rough Sleepers and the Keep Warm Pack Scheme.  The PHA has also committed funding towards the Complex Lives initiative, which brings together key stakeholders to provide support and pathways for those vulnerable service users in Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to breakdown the areas experiencing (a) the highest housing need; and (b) the highest housing stress; and (ii)  to detail the supply of housing units to each of these areas.
answer_text: The provision of a breakdown of the areas experiencing the highest housing need, the highest housing stress and the supply of housing units in these areas are operational matters for the Housing Executive.You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether funding to groups within the 36 Neighbourhood Renewal areas will be continued after 31 March 2024.
answer_text: A Budget has not been set for Departments for the 2024/25 financial year.  Discussions on what will be a challenging budget position are ongoing and funding for specific programmes such as Neighbourhood Renewal will become clearer when departmental allocations are finalised.The Department recognises the role that the voluntary and community sector plays in delivering critical services, support and advice to local communities and is committed to providing funding to the sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what steps her Department is taking to ensure that women, who may not be able to access Legal Aid, get support to take non-molestation orders to protect themselves and their families from domestic violence.
answer_text: There is an existing provision in regulation 10 of the Civil Legal Services (Financial) Regulations (NI) 2015 which provides for a waiver of the legal aid financial eligibility limits for an individual applying for a Non-Molestation Order under Article 20 of the Domestic Violence (NI) Order 1998. The Director of Legal Aid Casework applies this waiver to any applicant for a Non-Molestation Order who would not otherwise qualify for legal aid. The waiver means that if an applicant’s weekly disposable income exceeds £234, there is a contribution payable of the excess plus £134. Also, if the disposable capital of the applicant exceeds £3,000, the contribution payable shall be £100. The Legal Services Agency periodically issues reminders to solicitors of the existence of this waiver. Last summer the Agency participated in a Law Society event which highlighted this waiver and how it operates.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on (i) the public land for public housing process; and (ii) how potential land has been identified to meet the demand in areas of most need.
answer_text: The Public Land for Housing Pilot Scheme, which closed three years ago, recommended that looking at land to support future housing supply should form a central part of the Housing Supply Strategy which is currently in draft awaiting approval.Northern Ireland Housing Executive are expanding their work on land assembly and involving local council planning officials.  The Department has recently approved a Housing Executive Land Acquisition business case for the identification and acquisition of land in public ownership to support the continued development of social housing through the Social Housing Development Programme.  The proposal, developed initially as a pilot, is to identify sufficient land to deliver 100 units in areas of acute housing need between 2023 and 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the continuation of funding for the Community Mental Health Navigators in the Royal Hospital’s Emergency Department.
answer_text: The initial proposal for the Community Navigators Project was to fund a two-year pilot to trial a proof-of-concept model from 2022/23 to 2023/24, through non-recurrent New Decade New Approach (NDNA) funding.  Unfortunately, despite ongoing engagement with the Northern Ireland Office regarding NDNA funding, no approval has been obtained.  Funding was provided by the Department of Finance in 2022/23 to enable the Community Navigators Project, and other mental health initiatives, to commence while NDNA funding continued to be sought.  The Department of Health allocated funding in the current financial year to support the second, and final, year of the project.  This was included as part of the wider budget provided to support this year’s Mental Health Strategy delivery plan, recognising that enhancing mental health crisis provision is a key priority.Regarding future funding provision, there is no funding identified within the Department to support this initiative beyond this current financial year.  However, Belfast Trust and relevant partners have been advised that any future bids for support would need to be submitted to SPPG and PHA for consideration within their ongoing work on the development of the regional crisis service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on continuation of funding for Neighbourhood Renewal projects; (ii) whether there will there be an uplift of funding to include rising (a) running costs; and (b) salaries for Neighbourhood Renewal workers; and (iii) whether future funding will be based on objective need criteria.
answer_text: No budget has been set for Departments for the 2024/25 financial year.  Discussions on what will be a challenging budget position are ongoing and funding for specific programmes such as Neighbourhood Renewal will become clearer when departmental allocations are finalised.The Department recognises the role that the voluntary and community sector plays in delivering critical services, support and advice to local communities and is committed to providing funding to the sector.It is important that future funding to tackle deprivation is based on clear evidence of objective need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on workforce planning for social services in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust area.
answer_text: Effective workforce planning is a key theme of the Health and Social Care Workforce Strategy 2026: Delivering for Our People.  The aim of the Strategy is to develop and, by 2026, sustainably fund, an optimum workforce model for the reconfigured health and social care services. This is essential to ensure we invest appropriately in our HSC workforces, which are our most valuable asset.  Given this question relates to an operational workforce planning for social services within Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) the Trust have advised that they have established a Social Care Workforce Steering Group and a Social Work Recruitment Retention Strategic Group, both of which are focused on stabilising the workforce to ensure the safe and effective delivery of high quality care.  Both groups report to the Trust Executive Team, People and Culture Committee and include members from Trade Unions, Social Workers, Managers, Human Resources and Finance personnel.In the last year, a key focus has been working to cease the use of agency social workers, which, whilst significant in creating more workforce stability, has not addressed the chronic shortage of available staff within the profession.  The BHSCT has also explored and utilised the implementation of a skills mix, through further development and implementation of a Social Work Assistant role, who can undertake non-statutory roles through the delegation framework.The vision of BHSCT is that they will be the employer of choice for social workers and social care workers, so that they have a sustainable social work and social care workforce both now and for the future.  The steering groups bring a focus to what is required to improve recruitment and retention and ensure action on all of the recurring workforce challenges and enable opportunities to develop collective solutions.Current work force planning and retention activity focuses on the following priorities: Social Work Recruitment and Retention Strategic Group:Organisation and workforce capacity;Enhancing leadership;Being just and open; andStaff experience and wellbeing.Social Care Workforce Steering Group:Leadership management;Learning and career development; andHealthy teams.Some of the improvements required are being addressed at a Trust level whilst others will require regional collaboration with key partners and will be aligned to the recommendations of the Department of Health’s Workforce Review recommendations.  The BHSCT will continue to collaborate with the Department of Health on developing guidance for safe staffing and are represented on the Department’s Social Work Workforce Implementation Board, chaired by the Chief Social Work Officer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the funding for respite services for children and young people with physical and learning disability, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Short breaks (respite) provide valuable opportunities for children with disability to spend time away from their parents / primary carers, whilst also allowing parents / primary carers to have a break from their caring responsibilities. Across Northern Ireland, short break (respite) support for children with disability is delivered by the five Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts in partnership with the community and voluntary sector. Access to and the frequency of Short breaks (respite) is dependent on individually assessed need.Short break (respite) provision may include overnight care in a short break residential unit or ‘Sharing the Care’ type short break (respite) services which are community based, for example, fostering support, befriending support services, community activities such as afterschool support / youth group activities / summer schemes. The short break (respite) service may be provided to children with disability directly by the HSC Trust, by a community organisation who may have a contract with the HSC Trust to deliver the service, or via Direct Payments to the primary carer to enable support to be sourced.High-level planned funding / expenditure in respect of short breaks (respite) services in Health and Social Care Trusts, for the Learning Disability and Physical Disability Health Programmes of Care (POCs) in 2023/24, is provided below.  This is sourced from the Strategic Resource Framework (SRF) Report, which provides a high-level split of planned expenditure at the beginning of each financial year.  It should be noted that planned expenditure for services in the Learning Disability and Physical Disability POCs incorporates both Children’s and Adults, therefore expenditure solely for Children’s services is not specifically fully identifiable. question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on a Programme for Government.
answer_text: We are committed to developing and agreeing a Programme for Government at pace. The Executive knows that financial stability is vital to a successful ambitious Programme for Government and has written to Prime Minister Sunak to make the case for our public finances to be placed on a sustainable footing. We will be considering priorities for inclusion in a Programme for Government in the coming weeks, and will update the Assembly with a more specific timeline as our financial position becomes more clear.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how much funding, from his Department, has been withdrawn from the Quaker Cottage Project; and (ii) to outline the reasons why the funding was withdrawn.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how many people in the North Belfast constituency are not in training, education or employment.
answer_text: The Labour Force Survey provides estimates of the number of people not in education, employment or training (NEET).In April to June 2022 the estimated number of people (aged 16 to 64) who were not in education, employment or training in the North Belfast constituency was 21,000.Estimates from the Labour Force Survey are subject to an associated sampling error that decreases as the sample size increases.  It is the nature of sampling variability that the smaller the group whose size is being estimated, the less precise that estimate will be. Estimates for Assembly Area will therefore have a higher sampling variability than estimates for the region as a whole.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the progress of the Integrated Care Partnership for North Belfast.
answer_text: The current focus for Integrated Care Partnerships (ICP) for North Belfast and across the region is to utilise their collective skills and expertise to provide input into the development of the Integrated Care System for Northern Ireland (ICSNI).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on additional services and support for patients with Metastatic Breast Cancer.
answer_text: Support for metastatic breast cancer patients in Northern Ireland generally follows that of the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence.  This ensures that people diagnosed with metastatic breast cancer, have access to the same range of pharmaceutical and other treatments available in other regions of the UK. I launched the NI Cancer Strategy in March 2022 and Action 39 made a specific commitment to ensure that all people with cancer, including those with metastatic breast cancer have access to Clinical Nurse Specialists throughout the entire care pathway.Implementation of this action will further enhance the support provided for those with metastatic breast cancer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide the number of in-patient beds, including their locations, for children and young people with complex disability and mental health needs, in each of the 18 constituencies.
answer_text: Inpatient services specifically for children and young people with learning disability and mental health needs are provided on a regional basis at the Iveagh Centre and Beechcroft Child & Adolescent Inpatient Unit respectively. Currently, the Iveagh Centre has six inpatient beds for children and young people with a learning disability and additional mental health needs, whilst Beechcroft has 33 inpatient beds for children and young people with mental health needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of the funding that his Department, including InvestNI, have made into the North Belfast Constituency, from January 2020 until September 2022.
answer_text: My Department does not record spend based on geographical area.  Likewise funding is not allocated by area, instead potential projects are assessed for support on an individual basis regardless of their location.  However, where a breakdown of funding for the North Belfast Constituency is held, I have included it below. 		Invest NI reports its performance on a financial year basis, with the year ending 31 March 2022 being the most recent available.  Invest NI provided 245 offers of support, totalling £9.86 million, to businesses within the North Belfast Constituency from January 2020 to 31 March 2022.  		Invest NI proactively publishes a full breakdown of financial support offered to every business on the NI Open Data online portal, which can be accessed from the following link: Invest NI Open Data.  In addition to on-going business support, Invest NI assisted the Northern Ireland Executive in the delivery of nine emergency Covid recovery schemes of which £29.09 million was paid to businesses in the North Belfast Constituency.  		From January 2020 until September 2022, Tourism Northern Ireland provided £444k of funding into the North Belfast Constituency via a range of capital programmes and tourism event funding. 		InterTradeIreland provided £184k of funding for businesses in the North Belfast Constituency between January 2020 and September 2022.  		Under Priorities One and Two of the Northern Ireland European Social Fund Programme, £14.32 million of funding was provided to projects in the North Belfast Constituency between January 2020 and September 2022.  The funding was inclusive of European Union assistance, provided along with Departmental funding.  However, it should be noted that many of these projects operate in other constituencies throughout Northern Ireland.  Furthermore, funding is provided to projects based outside the North Belfast Constituency which may be delivered, and benefits thereby accrued, in the North Belfast Constituency.		My Department has played a pivotal role in the successful delivery of the Ulster University's new Greater Belfast campus, including the provision of £126 million of capital funding for the project since January 2020.  This funding was made up of a £25 million capital grant and a £101 million Financial Transactions Capital Loan.  In addition, funding was provided to the Ulster University in its entirety.  A breakdown of spend per campus is not held by my Department.		My Department provides funding to meet the day to day running costs and capital for our six Further Education colleges.  Between January 2020 and September 2022 the Northern Regional College’s Newtownabbey Campus received £811k capital funding for minor works projects.  It is not possible to provide the exact spend for the Newtownabbey Campus, as it is not allocated or held on a campus basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what follow up work has been undertaken in Clifton Care Home in North Belfast since the publication of the Independent Review in April 2022.
answer_text: I can confirm that following the publication of the Independent Review in April 2022 that the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) has maintained City View Court Nursing Home (previously known as Clifton Nursing Home) on a level of escalation.This escalation process involves monthly quality assurance visits by BHSCT which includes seeking service user and carer feedback. This arrangement is in addition to the ongoing individual reviews and support provided to residents and their families.Senior BHSCT representatives have shared the SAI report with families who engaged in the review process and have convened meetings with those families who wished to further discuss the findings and recommendations.An action plan has been developed to oversee the implementation of the thirty-six recommendations arising from the Independent Review. This action plan continues to be progressed through fortnightly BHSCT Senior Management oversight meetings and includes representation from City View Court management. BHSCT staff continue to have a presence at City View Court to observe care delivery and the Trust continues to work closely with RQIA and City View Court management to ensure the care home has the right support to implement the required improvements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many children and young people, from North Belfast, are on the child protection register; (ii) what the caseload is for each social worker; and (iii) do the caseloads include children with disabilities.
answer_text: As of 28 September 2022, there were 140 children residing in the North Belfast area on the child protection register in the Belfast Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust.Within Children’s Community Services in the Belfast HSC Trust, the size of each social worker’s caseload is dependent on the service area in which they work and the remit of their interventions, although factors such as years of experience and complexity of cases are also considered. Social work teams can be located throughout the Trust and their caseloads are not always aligned to specific geographical areas.The Belfast HSC Trust has a dedicated Children with Disabilities Service within Children’s Community Services, and who work exclusively with children and young people who have a diagnosed disability; however, social workers aligned to other service areas may also have children on their caseload who have a disability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how public funds paid to the private health sector are monitored for equality and other data duties.
answer_text: To date, £69m has been allocated in 2022/23 for additional waiting list activity for the period April to December. The funding is being used to help reduce waiting times for assessment and treatment across a range of services. The funding is being targeted at those patients with the highest clinical priority and involves creating additional in-house health service capacity over and above normal day to day work, as well as paying independent sector providers to assess and treat patients who are on waiting lists.With regard to this funding, all new referrals, appointments and outpatient waiting lists are managed according to clinical priorities. Priorities must be identified for each patient on the waiting list and allocated according to urgency of the treatment. Trusts manage patients in three priorities, i.e. red flag (suspect cancer), urgent and routine. The responsible contracting authority in advance of a contract award to an independent sector provider will have specified their requirements for the control and processing of data in line with regulations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many Bank and Agency Staff are working in the Iveagh Centre as of 16 September 2022.
answer_text: Iveagh is currently experiencing staffing difficulties and is having to manage associated risks. In some cases, this has led to the decision to release children residing in Iveagh into community placements. Belfast Trust keep staffing arrangements under review to ensure patient safety and care quality. Iveagh continues to have sufficient staff to provide assessment and treatment to patients.One child was released from the Iveagh Centre in the week beginning 13 September 2022 due to a sudden escalation of staff availability. Unfortunately, only 24 hours’ notice was provided. A crisis management plan was developed by the team in Iveagh and community team. As of 16 September 2022, there were 19 staff unavailable to work in Iveagh due to short term and unplanned reasons.  Subsequently, 2 staff have returned to work on the week commencing 26th September and plans are in place to restore staffing fully.Staff were sought from across the Trust, Nurse Bank Staff and Agency staff with the level of skills required to work with the young people with complex needs however the numbers achieved did not meet safe staffing levels.  Support was also sought and gained from Senior Nursing Managers and Allied Health Professionals, who provided direct patient care but this was not sustainable. At present, four agency staff work regular shifts in the Iveagh Centre. Iveagh ward currently has one Registered Nurse vacancy and four senior nursing assistant vacancies. These vacancies will be filled via normal recruitment processes. Recruitment for Iveagh has not been an historical issue and these vacancies have only occurred in the last month.One child was re-admitted to Iveagh following trial at home release on 17 September 2022. The child was subsequently moved to a community placement on 21 September 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether every Health Trust is providing early medical abortion services.
answer_text: Belfast Trust, Northern Trust, Southern Trust and South Eastern Trusts are providing an early medical abortion service. WHSCT is working towards the restart of its early medical abortion service for week beginning 3 October 2022. Until that date, women and girls who live in the Western Trust area and who are seeking an early medical abortion can contact the British Pregnancy Advisory Service (BPAS) and they will be referred to another Trust in the interim for an early medical abortion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to confirm how much money has been spent on Agency Staff since January 2020 until the end of August 2022 for each Health Trust and to provide a breakdown of Agency Staff spending for each Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether each of the Health Trusts have a Winter Surge Plan; and (ii) will social services be included in each Health Trust’s plan.
answer_text: Trusts have submitted Service Delivery Plans for 2022/23 which require them to return to pre pandemic levels of service based on what they delivered on a month by month basis in 2019/20. These levels of activity would have been provided throughout the Winter period and so factor in seasonal pressures such as influenza, other unscheduled pressures and focusing on maximising delivery of elective care. The high impact indicators that the Trusts are being monitored against are consistent with the approach taken by NHS England to manage seasonal pressures and include measures to reduce hospital occupancy, ensure timely discharge, provide better support for people at home, and improve community response for urgent and emergency care. The service delivery plans do include a range of indicators across adult and children’s social services as well as mental health services. In addition to Trust provided services, the Plan also includes indicators around primary care services including Community Pharmacy and GP Out of Hours

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) what actions her Department has taken to support Education Welfare Officers; and (ii) when the review of the Education Welfare Officer role will be completed.
answer_text: DE officials hold regular liaison meetings with Education Welfare Service (EWS) colleagues to provide support and policy guidance. Officials also participate on the Education Transformation Project Board and relevant work streams. Education Welfare Officers, in turn, receive regular supervision from Senior Education Welfare Officers in line with Northern Ireland Social Care (NISC) requirements.Subject to final Project Board approval, the Education Authority (EA) intend to publish the final EWS project report by 31 December 2022.  The final timing of this will be dependent on consultation with staff, the teaching and non-teaching trade unions and approval by EA governance structures.
