question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what actions her Department has taken to ensure that pupils of Mercy College in North Belfast have public transport to their school.
answer_text: A privately funded arrangement between Mercy College and the Flax trust providing free bus transport for all pupils since 2017 ceased during the course of the last academic year.  The vast majority of the pupils are not eligible for transport assistance under the Department’s home to school transport policy, as they live within 3 miles of the school or are passing a school of the same category within 3 miles of home.  This means in effect, the Department is unable to provide support under the current policy to most of these pupils and is unable to fund a dedicated bus service.Since the privately funded transport arrangement ceased last year, my Department has worked with the Council for the Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS), the managing authority for the school, the Education Authority and Translink to seek to ensure adequate public transport arrangements are available for those pupils who choose to use it, as fare paying passengers.  I understand that Translink has been able to make some adjustments to services in the area to provide a more dedicated transport arrangement for Mercy College pupils as fare paying passengers on two of the routes to school.  My Department will continue to provide assistance and support to all of the agencies involved in these discussions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what steps his Department is taking to ensure that the cost of living crisis is mitigated for all Health and Social Care staff.
answer_text: I recently publicly acknowledged the need to ensure all staff are appropriately paid, when highlighting the financial pressures and budgetary uncertainty facing our health service. I must stress that our pay award decisions can also only be taken in the context of a wider public sector pay policy set by the Department of Finance. Any pay increase will also require to be fully funded by the Executive, given the financial outlook facing my Department in 2022/23.I further issued a statement on 20th July, in which I set out that I have considered the recommendations made by the NHSPRB and DDRB, and would be content to recommend acceptance of the relevant awards. In relation to the Agenda for Change pay award, this would maintain the previous commitments made by the Executive to maintain pay parity with England.However, unlike my colleagues in other parts of the United Kingdom, I am unable to announce the immediate implementation of these pay awards locally as Northern Ireland still does not have an agreed Executive Budget for 2022/23. Equally, I welcome and support the recent letter from the three devolved Finance Ministers asking the previous Chancellor to increase budgets to address pay and other pressures, including in the Health Service.In order to help healthcare staff with the increased cost of fuel when travelling for work purposes, I have approved an increase to the secondary rate on annual mileage over 3,500 miles to bring it up to 30p per mile, representing a 50% increase on the previous rate of 20p per mile; benefitting those who are required to use their vehicles most frequently.The current cost of living crisis impacts on us all, yet Departments cannot address all issues in isolation – we need a functioning Executive to deliver appropriate support across our society.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why the Education Authority has not secured taxi services to schools for children who have a Special Educational Needs Statement in North Belfast.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) provides transport assistance for over 90,000 pupils each day.  In the region of 9,000 of these pupils have a statement of Special Educational Need (SEN) with approximately 37% of those pupils travelling by taxi. There are approximately 1,600 taxi routes operating each day.The EA has advised me that it is facing additional challenges this year in providing taxi transport assistance to pupils with a statement of Special Educational Needs, with the greater Belfast area having the largest concentration of SEN pupils and therefore facing the most disruption. These challenges include a 40% reduction in taxi drivers in Northern Ireland from 2013-2021 which has led to diminishing market capacity, and the rise of fuel prices which has further exacerbated industry challenges.  The complexity and length of some of the journeys can also make them unattractive to operators.  These difficulties led to a number of contracts being handed back in the 48hrs leading up to services recommencing this year as operators who had successfully bid for tenders were subsequently unable to secure drivers to fulfil the contracted runs.  The EA assures me that its transport staff continue to work tirelessly to find solutions for pupils in all areas experiencing difficulties with transport. By Monday 5 September it had resolved the transport issues for 84% of those pupils affected by the handback of taxi contracts on Friday 2 September. The EA Transport helpline will remain open until 16th September and the EA will continue to engage with parents impacted by lack of transport services and offer alternative provision which may include a parental payment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans his Department has to provide services aimed at eliminating drug overdoses.
answer_text: Last September, I launched Northern Ireland's new 10-year Substance Use Strategy "Preventing Harm & Empowering Recovery: A Strategic Framework to Tackle Substance Use". Underscored by five population-level outcomes, the vision of the new strategy is that people in Northern Ireland are supported in the prevention and reduction of the harm related to the use and misuse of alcohol and other drugs, and will be empowered to maintain recovery.One of the main actions is for the Public Health Agency (PHA) and the Strategic Planning & Performance Group (SPPG) to develop an Overdose & Relapse Prevention Framework. In addition, there are a range of specific actions within the strategy that place emphasis on harm reduction support measures, including expanding the supply of Naloxone to reverse overdoses, and developing the accessibility of Low Threshold Services for people who use alcohol and other drugs. Other actions within the strategy specify working with service users and their families to support the development of recovery communities, as well as supporting the involvement of experts by experience, service users and their families at all levels in the implementation of this strategy.Finally, the PHA and SPPG are currently developing a new comprehensive, whole-system strategic plan for substance use services and support, which will address demographic trends in increasing demand and complexity, current and future resource, current gaps in service provision, service pressures, workforce issues and equity of access to support across the Region.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many disability social workers are based in North Belfast.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social (HSC) Trust stated that at 8th August 2022, there were 5 WTE Disability Social Workers working in the North Belfast team.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what psychological support is provided to the former patients and relatives of deceased former patients of Dr. Michael Watt.
answer_text: The Belfast Trust secured psychological services for Cohorts 1, 2 and 3 to support patients with psychological and emotional issues arising from involvement in the neurology recall process.  Patients who attended the Neurology Recall continue to have access to psychological support where this has been requested by individual patients themselves or through a referral from a clinician.The Belfast Trust have also secured an independent provider, Inspire Wellbeing, to offer support to patients who may not wish to avail of the psychological services provided directly by the Trust.  Patients can access services by contacting the Belfast Trust Neurology Advice Line (0800 980 1100). Throughout Phase Two of the Expert Review of Records of Deceased Patients of Dr Watt, families have been supported by the Family Liaison Team established by the RQIA. This has included families being supported by a clinical psychologist during face-to-face meetings with the Family Liaison Team. Support materials on bereavement and coping with grief, specific to this Review, have also been produced and provided to all affected families. The Family Liaison Team remains accessible to family members and will continue to offer additional emotional and psychological support, such as the Inspire Wellbeing independent counselling service, for families who wish to avail of this. The RQIA Family Liaison Team can be contacted by email to expert.review@rqia.org.uk or by telephone on 0800 052 0012.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what funds were given to support local GPs between April 2021 and April 2022; (ii) what additional funds his Department intends to make to ensure continuity of services; and (iii) what measures have been taken to address the workforce planning challenges with GPs.
answer_text: My Department paid a total of £360.3m funding to support local GP practices between 1st April 2021 and 31st March 2022.I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care, and as Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available. My ability to plan strategically is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty.  As a result, it is expected that funding pressures across our health and social care services may be significant by the second half of 2022/23.  My Department’s workforce planning approach has sought to take account of the need to ensure that we have a GP workforce that is supported, motivated and sustainable and that continues to provide quality care to patients. The following measures have been taken to assist with the ongoing workforce challenges:   GP Training places number 111 per annum from 2019/20 an increase of 70% increase from 2015 levels. This has been further boosted by an additional 10 GP training places for the 2022/23 academic year as an interim measure bringing training places to an all-time high of 121;The GP Induction and Refresher Scheme provides opportunities for GPs who meet the required criteria to safely return to general practice following a career break or time spent working abroad. It also supports the safe introduction of GPs who may have qualified overseas and provides support to GPs returning to or entering clinical practice in Northern Ireland.  The GP Retention Scheme is designed to assist in the retention of GPs who may be considering leaving or reducing their sessional commitment to general practice. GPs also benefit from being part of a wider primary care team.  My Department continues to work to introduce the wider roll-out of primary care Multi-disciplinary Teams (MDTs), Advanced Nurse Practitioners and additional General Practice Nurses, all of which are making a difference to how services are delivered in primary care and contributing to improved patient outcomes. Since its launch in 2018, my Department has allocated over £57m to the MDT Programme. There are currently in 339.41 whole time equivalent front line staff working across 101 GP practices in 7 GP Federation areas in the Primary Care MDT programme.I am committed to extending the MDT model as quickly as possible; however, the speed at which this can be achieved will depend on the availability of appropriate funding (including capital funding) and suitably qualified and experienced staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department and the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust will address the 76 recommendations arising from the Independent Neurology Inquiry.
answer_text: The Department is currently ensuring the Report of the Independent Neurology Inquiry receives the careful consideration it deserves, and appropriate governance arrangements will be put in place to ensure the recommendations are appropriately implemented. I intend to provide more detail on these arrangements in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has any plans to increase mileage allowance for Health and Social Care staff who do not cross the 3,500 mile threshold.
answer_text: My Department has no immediate plans to the increase the mileage allowance for Health and Social Care staff who do not cross the 3,500 mile threshold. I have recently announced that staff who exceed the 3,500 mile threshold will receive 30p for each additional mile above the threshold.  My decision has been taken at risk, without an Executive in place and despite the ongoing uncertainty in regard to the overall Health budget for 2022/23. I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care and will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources available. This revised rate will be subject to review at the end of September.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the families of deceased neurology patients will have sight of the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority and Royal College of Physicians' report before prior to publication.
To ask the Minister of Health on what date Health and Social Care staff will be able to use fuel cards to pay for mileage expenses.
answer_text: In response to rising fuel costs, I have considered a range of options to help staff, including fuel cards. Governance arrangements, which would be required to avoid inadvertently creating individual tax liabilities, would mean the use of fuel cards would not be an immediate solution. I announced that I will increase the rate paid for mileage incurred above 3,500 miles to 30p per mile for an initial six month period, to be reviewed. This measure will benefit those who are required to use their vehicles most frequently.My decision has been taken at risk, without an Executive in place and despite the ongoing uncertainty in regard to the overall Health budget for 2022/23. I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care and will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources available.I have also committed to additional staff in BSO Payroll to carry out manual processing of eligible staff and this means payments for staff may begin to be processed this month.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has plans to ensure that the contracts given to the Independent Health Sector have the same terms and conditions for domiciliary care, as those of Health and Social Care staff.
answer_text: Establishing a fair, standardised system for pay, and terms and conditions is complex in a mixed economy of care system with a large amount of employers. However, I believe that improved pay, and terms and conditions for the lowest paid are central to future reform of the system.In regards to any new Domiciliary Care contracts, Procurement Policy Note ‘PPN 01/21 Scoring Social Value’ states that from June 2022 “the payment of the living wage must be included as a condition of contract for all tenders”.  To comply with this requirement, the payment of the living wage will be included in the Terms & Conditions of Contract for all future service tenders including Domiciliary Care tenders.For independent providers, the Department cannot mandate what terms and conditions are offered to employees. However, at the business case stage and the procurement stage, the contracting authority will determine all legislative and policy requirements in relation to this sector and any requirements will be clearly built into the contract documents. As you will be aware, last year I announced a £23m funding support package to the domiciliary, and wider care sector.  There was a significant amount of engagement between the sector and Department officials on this funding package, with a clear recognition that there is a need to ensure this additional funding goes to the front line staff.While the Department cannot mandate what levels of pay, or terms and conditions that independent employers should offer, in general terms, this support package was provided with the expectation that it would be used to improve terms and conditions for employees, as well as stabilising and enhancing capacity across the sector. Despite a very challenging financial climate I have ensured that sufficient funding was provided to Trusts to ensure this uplift was carried through to the current year and made recurrent.As you may be aware, a public consultation recently closed which sought views relating to long term proposals on the Reform of Adult Social Care. The theme of a valued workforce is considered within the proposals put forward. Whilst the proposals on the Reform of Adult Social Care will see wide ranging changes to the delivery of social care, this reform package will require sustained additional investment and budgetary certainty.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether it is illegal to burn tyres; (ii) whether his Department will remove tyres from the bonfire at Herron Way, Waterside; and (iii) whether his Department will remove tyres from other bonfire sites.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 1206/22-27, how many children and young people who have had their first Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service appointment are now on a waiting list to access the services to which they were referred.
answer_text: My Department does not routinely collect figures on the number of children and young people waiting to access the services to which they were referred following their first Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service (CAMHS) assessment; therefore, this information has been requested from each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust.The Belfast HSC Trust have informed my Department there are 557 children and young people on a waiting list to access the services to which they were referred following their first CAMHS assessment. As the Belfast HSC Trust also provides this service for the South Eastern HSC Trust, this figure includes 221 children and young people in the Belfast sector and 336 in South Eastern sector.The Northern HSC Trust could not provide a response as it would require a manual count of referrals at a disproportionate cost. In the Southern HSC Trust, there are currently no children or young people within CAMHS sitting on internal waiting lists. The Western HSC Trust have not provided information on the number of children and young people who are on a waiting list to access the CAMHS to which they were referred, but state that relevant psychoeducation and resources are available while the child or young person is waiting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 1207/22-27, what options he is considering for increasing the mileage for domiciliary care workers; and when will he make a decision on these options.
answer_text: I recognise the difficulties faced by our healthcare staff due to rising fuel costs and I have approved a 50% increase to the secondary rate on annual mileage over 3,500 miles to bring it up to 30p per mile. This measure, which will be for an initial six month period, to be reviewed, will benefit those who are required to use their vehicles most frequently.My Officials have already engaged in discussions with Trust and BSO colleagues to discuss feasibility of fuel cards, or any other process by which payment may be expedited to staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Duty of Candour for all Health and Social Care staff will be brought forward as legislation within this mandate.
answer_text: Following the completion of a public consultation in 2021, the Duty of Candour Workstream provided its assessment to me in February 2022.  In response, I have asked my officials to continue to develop the policy for a "Being Open Framework" for our health and social care system. My officials will also carry out further study on how a duty of candour might work in practice, including additional analysis of the impact of such a duty on the health and care service, both legally, and in workforce terms.  It is my intention that the introduction of any legislation should support an open, honest and just culture without creating unintended negative consequences.  Officials will continue to engage with key stakeholders to develop a greater understanding of these issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many respite facilities are open to families or carers in the North Belfast Constituency; and (ii) to provide the (a) location of; and (b) services available at, each facility.
answer_text: There are currently three facilities open to service users from North Belfast. Please see the table below showing detailing the services available at each facility.Respite facilitiesLocationServices availableRigby Close8 Rigby Close, BT15 5JF2 beds with 1 currently open for respite.  The other is opening in July 2022.  This is a full respite service with tracking hoist for adults with complex physical health needs.Trench Park28 Trench Park, BT11 9FG2 beds both currently open for respite.  The service provides full respite services including care for adults with complex physical health needs.Mertoun Park17 Orchard Court, Holywood, BT4 2WD7 beds with 5 currently open.  The other 2 beds are opening on the 22nd of July.  This unit provides respite for a range of service users with access to transport for outings.  There is 1 bed available for adults with complex physical health problems.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to provide a breakdown of how many post-primary school places are available in the (a) maintained sector; and (b) controlled sector, in North Belfast ; and (ii) to list the post-primary schools that have available places in North Belfast.
answer_text: There are no remaining Year 8 places in the Maintained sector for September 2022 admission in North Belfast, with 47 places remaining in the Controlled sector.The schools with available places remaining at Year 8 are Belfast Model School for Girls and Glengormley High School (HS).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what assurances he can give to people waiting on the Neurology Redress Scheme, that they will not be disadvantaged by the length of time it is taking his Department to provide redress.
answer_text: Neurology recall compensation arrangements were announced by Written Assembly Statement, on 9 June 2022. The announcement was supported by a neurology recall stakeholder engagement meeting, facilitated by the Patient and Client Council (PCC). Concern was raised regarding individuals who were expecting a redress scheme may be disadvantaged by the standard 3 year timeframe to initiate a clinical negligence claim. This issue is being considered and will be addressed through the formal PCC engagement platform.  The outcome of the compensation work has highlighted the need to support a fair resolution and remedy for patients and families seeking compensation. Each neurology recall case will therefore be considered on its own merit to ensure a just outcome for the patients and families affected, and to ensure that, where the legal threshold is met, in appropriate cases claimants receive the compensation they deserve.  Neurology recall claims are complex and it is difficult to make a commitment on the time it will take to complete cases. A dedicated neurology recall legal team is in place and a streamlined pathway has been developed to ensure cases can progress as quickly as possible.  The Departments Neurology Compensation Project Board will continue to oversee and assess the progression of this work to ensure that cases progress as quickly as possible, in a compassionate, fair and reasonable manner.The Department cannot provide legal advice to neurology recall patients and families who should seek appropriate independent legal advice if they wish to pursue a compensation claim.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many Red Flag patients in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust are waiting for surgery.
answer_text: As at 31st March 2022, there were 519 “Red Flag” patients waiting for inpatient or day case admission for surgery in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust. “Red Flag” patients have been defined as those with an expected method of admission relating to "Suspect Cancer”.The majority of patients waiting for diagnostic endoscopic procedures are excluded from this figure however there may be a small number of other non-surgical and diagnostic procedures included. Patients are reported against the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust as being responsible for the service providing their treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many children and young people are waiting for their first appointment with Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services.
answer_text: The latest information available shows that at 30 April 2022, there were 2,223 children and young people waiting to access Children and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to increase the mileage allowance for domiciliary care workers, given the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: In light of the recent significant increases in fuel costs, my Department, Employers and Trade Unions have been involved in national and local discussions regarding a potential review of the current Agenda for Change (AfC) Mileage Rates.The national discussions have been led through the NHS Staff Council and have been exploring the options in relation to mileage reimbursement rates. Unfortunately it has not been possible to conclude a national agreement on an increase in fuel reimbursement rates.In light of no nationally agreed position, temporary increases have been agreed in Scotland and Wales until the end of July and June respectively. At the time of those agreements, no decision could be made locally due to election period restrictions. My Department is, however, considering options for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to list any Housing Associations that are asking tenants for deposits before taking possession of an allocated social home.
answer_text: I have been assured that registered Housing Associations and the Housing Executive do not require a deposit before commencement of a tenancy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust are waiting for surgery.
answer_text: As at 31st March 2022, there were 40,810 patients waiting for inpatient or day case admission for surgery in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust. The majority of patients waiting for diagnostic endoscopic procedures are excluded from this figure however there may be a small number of other non-surgical and diagnostic procedures included. Patients are reported against the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust as being responsible for the service providing their treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many children are waiting for a multi-disciplinary team meeting in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust to provide an autism diagnosis.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to bring forward a funded strategy for menopause within this mandate.
answer_text: Over recent months I have been participating in the UK Menopause Taskforce, meeting with Ministerial counterparts and colleagues from the other UK jurisdictions, as well as experts within the menopause field. The Taskforce was established to strengthen coordination and raise awareness of the menopause and its impact across the UK, under the shared aim of improving care and support for women experiencing the menopause across all sectors of society. I recognise that menopause affects women across all sectors of society, and if the Northern Ireland Executive is supportive of cross-Departmental work in this area, my Department would, of course, contribute to this as required. In the meantime, in addition to the majority of menopause services being provided through GPs and the regional HRT clinic in Belfast, there are a number of service developments under way in other Trusts thanks to staff working in the gynaecology field who have a special interest in menopause. For example, a specialist clinic has recently been established in the Antrim Area Hospital. Sustained funding would be required to place these developments on a firmer footing, to build on the work of the regional HRT clinic, and to enhance awareness amongst healthcare staff and the general public around perimenopause and menopause, and on how to support women at this time of their lives.With regard to funding, I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care.  As Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available.  Amid ongoing budget uncertainty, I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health may be significant by the second half of 2022/23. The financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement.  My ability to plan strategically is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty. Funding elements of the health service on a month to month or quarter to quarter basis is not an efficient or effective way to build for the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action is being taken to reduce the time that patients are waiting in ambulances before being seen in Emergency Departments.
answer_text: Urgent and emergency care services have been under significant and increasing pressure for over a decade.  The delayed handover of patients to Emergency Departments (EDs) is a symptom of the wider pressures across the system.  A number of short and longer term actions are under way to help to address these system-wide pressures.My Department’s strategic response is the Review of Urgent and Emergency Care and a public consultation on proposals to reform these services, which I launched on 16 March 2022, has recently concluded.I believe the proposed reforms will help to ensure that all citizens in Northern Ireland have equal access to safe urgent and emergency care services, tailored to their specific needs, at the right time and in the right place.While work on the Review was impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic, I published the No More Silos Action Plan in October 2020, which included 10 key actions to ensure that urgent and emergency care services across primary and secondary care could be maintained and improved in the short to medium term in an environment that is safe for patients and for staff.  The key initiatives which have laid the foundations for the reform of urgent and emergency care included: the introduction of Phone First services in Northern, Southern and Western Trusts, with interim services also available in Downe and Lagan Valley Hospitals; the introduction of Urgent Care Centres; the Timely Discharge Initiative; emergency department Ambulance Handover Bays; and enhanced investment in Hospital at Home services.  Collectively, these actions help to improve patient flow, ease congestion at emergency departments, and provide alternatives to emergency department or hospital admission for patients who require urgent treatment but are not facing an immediately life threatening illness.  A total of £21.2 million was invested in the No More Silos Action Plan during 2021/22.  However, I accept that ambulance turnaround times continue to be challenging in some areas and at times of peak demand.  My Department is continuously working with the Public Health Agency, the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) and the five other Health and Social Care Trusts to address waiting times at our emergency departments, enhance the flow of patients through the system, facilitate timely discharge and coordinate better management of our ambulance resources, including minimising the time that patients wait in ambulances for handover.To assist with this, the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service has increased the number of Hospital Ambulance Liaison Officers working at emergency departments whose role is to expedite the handover of patients and the turnaround of ambulances to respond to calls waiting.  There are currently 12 Hospital Ambulance Liaison Officers operating across a number of hospitals.  There are environmental upgrades to increase physical space and handover zones being created at a number of hospitals, and work is continuing in this area.  In

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many individuals or couples have seen an adoption panel in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust since 1 January 2020; (ii) how many staff shortages there are in Social Work within the Trust; and (iii) to outline the plan to fill those staff shortages.
answer_text: (i) 31 applicants attended an Adoption Panel in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust between 1 January 2020 and 26 May 2022. (ii) There are currently 72 full time equivalent social work vacancies across all Programmes of Care in the Trust.(iii) Currently all Trusts are collectively recruiting to vacant Social Work posts through regional recruitment processes.  In addition, in an effort to address pressures in the immediate term, there have been recent regional workforce appeals for retired Social Work staff and those not in statutory work to help Trusts on a part-time/bank basis.   The South Eastern Trust also seeks to proactively attract final year social work students and to support and develop its workforce. My Department recently published a Review of the Social Work workforce, to ensure there is sufficient capacity to deliver sustainable social work services across all fields of social work, and able to meet the demands of a transformed Health and Social Care Service.  An Implementation Board has been established to oversee the delivery of the Review’s recommendations which will address a number of workforce issues, including commissioning, recruitment, retention and the development of the workforce.  The South Eastern Trust is actively engaged in the delivery of the Review’s recommendations.An independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services is currently underway and will consider the impact of social work staffing vacancies and workloads on the delivery of children's social care services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many Special Recognition Payments will be made to the independent health and social care sector before the end of June 2022.
answer_text: Phase 2 had been temporarily paused to allow officials to seek clarity on a number of issues raised relating to the impact of the increase in national insurance contributions which are included as part of the payment provided to the former employer.  A further letter was issued to employers on 14 April 2022 providing clarity on the issues raised and confirming recommencement of the payment process. Claims are being processed by BSO in date order when all relevant information is provided by the claimants. No date has been set for the closure of the scheme and claims will continue to be processed by the Business Services Organisation during 2022/23. At this stage, I am not in a position to quantify the number of payments that will be made by the end of June 2022. I can confirm that as at 18 May 2022, a total of over £16.6m has been paid out under Phase 1 to independent sector care home and domiciliary care providers covering 28,457 individuals. As at the same date over £2.8m has been paid out to 6,847 individuals under Phase 2; these payments include staff previously employed in independent sector care home and domiciliary care providers who have since moved employer or left the sector, and staff employed with eligible day opportunity providers and buildings-based day services.In addition we have also opened the Scheme to eligible applicants in the following groups, Personal Assistants employed by Independent Living Fund recipients, Thalidomide Health Grant recipients, and Personal Assistants employed by those in receipt of Direct Payments from HSC Trusts. This element of the scheme was launched on 23 March and is being delivered by ILF Scotland for the payments, Scotland Excel who set up and run the online portal, and SDS Scotland who manage the dedicated helpline. These delivery partners were heavily involved in delivering the equivalent scheme for the Scottish Government.  To date £300k has been paid to 863 individuals. To encourage uptake on this element Trusts have now written to all individuals in receipt of direct payments. My officials are also finalising arrangements to include eligible staff whose employers are no longer trading and I hope to be in a position to include this cohort of individuals in the coming weeks.A FAQ on the Special Recognition Payment has been published on the Department’s website as below:-https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/news/hsc-staff-recognition-payment-faqsThis continues to be updated with relevant information as each phase of the claims process progresses. I remain personally committed to ensuring the recognition payment will be paid to independent sector staff as soon as it is practicable.
